name: The Connector
intro: As a CONNECTOR, you rely on Connective Listening most. You listen for information that might be useful for others and their situations. You will tend to tune out when there is not a human component to the information shared.
shortDescription: Connectors rely on Connective Listening most. They listen for information that might be useful for others and their situations. They will tend to tune out when there is not a human component to the information shared.
strengths: With your high preference for Connective Listening, you are most comfortable when orienting information to relationships and personal interactions. You naturally provide support and empathy toward others. You also appreciate a broad application of information beyond your own self- interest, and respect the knowledge, experience, and interests of the people around you. When others are speaking, you tend to listen for the feelings that lie behind the facts.
possibleChallenges: You might grow impatient when the interests of others are not being factored into discussions that could impact them. You may also tend to overlook facts and data in your decision-making, and accept information at face value, especially if it comes from a trusted or likeable source. You can be ruled by emotions rather than reason, and may forget to look inward toward what is personally relevant.
insights: Listen to what is being said as well as who is saying it—do not get co-opted by the speaker.
Take time to reflect on information rather than rushing to action with limited knowledge.
Ask fact-based and detailed questions that show rational thinking.
Use body language, voice and eye contact to show engagement without overwhelming the speaker(s).
Ask yourself how you might apply the information being presented to a problem that you’re tasked to resolve.

CV.atBest: Due to your mutually relational nature, you will tend to get along easily, each listening to the other with interest. Your likely openness toward each other will encourage fresh thinking and creative ideas to be explored without judgment.
CV.challenges: Your mutual strength could become your mutual liability if your interest in connecting causes you to ignore other relevant facts or necessary practicalities. You each could falsely assume that because of similar thinking, you’re on the right track.
CV.suggestions: Watch out for any tendency to avoid addressing diﬃcult facts in order to maintain ease in the relationship. Ground your conversations in facts, data, or knowledge from previous experiences.

RV.atBest: Reflective Listeners will enrich the exchange with useful insights and ideas based on their knowledge and experience. You can help pull out their thoughts and ideas due to the natural sense of trust you establish.
RV.challenges: Because you like feeling connected to others, you may become uncomfortable with Reflective Listeners who are slow to offer their contributions. Since you are poised to act, and Reflective Listeners are poised to consider, they may feel overwhelmed with your desire to connect before they have a chance to process their own thoughts and opinions.
RV.suggestions: Give Reflective Listeners plenty of time and room to process new information before having to offer back their input. To encourage their engagement, ask Reflective Listeners if they've had previous experiences that would be applicable to the current situation.

AL.atBest: Analytical Listeners can help you ground your thinking in facts and data. You, in turn, can help Analytical Listeners consider how their analyses and researched opinions will “land” with others.
AL.challenges: Given your divergent priorities, you may tune out Analytical Listeners who stick to facts and details without considering a “people” component. Analytical Listeners may consider your prioritization of other people to be impractical or unrealistic and may probe your ideas to see if they hold up under scrutiny.
AL.suggestions: Try not to take the Analytical Listener’s probing as a statement of personal mistrust. Rather, value them as a counterweight that brings balance to your thinking. Back up your ideas with facts and data when speaking to Analytical Listeners. If you do, they will be more likely to see the importance of your thinking.

CL.atBest: You’ll tend to offer Conceptual Listeners room to be themselves and ideate, while making sure their ideas are taking all possible constituencies into consideration. Conceptual Listeners, in turn, can help take your people-oriented ideas to imaginative levels you hadn’t considered.
CL.challenges: Together, you may share a good “brainstorming” rapport but it may also lack grounding in facts, details, and practicality. You may find a tension growing when the Conceptual Listener’s interest in ideas and possibilities conflicts with your interest in people and processes.
CL.suggestions: Try to keep an agenda to the conversation that has a structure and clearly defined goals to curb the possibility of drifting into prolonged ideating. While you'll naturally speak of ideas and how they might impact people, try also to round out your brainstorming with facts, data, and lessons from previous experiences wherever possible.

name: The Interactor
intro: As an INTERACTOR, you rely on Connective Listening in combination with Reflective Listening. You listen for ways knowledge and experience will benefit others. You will tend to tune out when those people who are able to use what has been learned are not engaged.
shortDescription: Interactors rely on Connective Listening in combination with Reflective Listening. They listen for ways knowledge and experience will benefit others. They will tend to tune out when those people who are able to use what has been learned are not engaged.
strengths: You focus on the meaning of information for others as well as for yourself. You pay attention to what others have to offer, but take time to reflect on the information. You thrive in groups as well as one-on-one interactions, and carve out time for both public and private engagement. You most value relationships with others where their knowledge and experience can be used.
possibleChallenges: Too much time with others or alone can be equally wearing; you have to balance two competing voices, others and self, when making decisions. You can get caught between wanting to share information with others and holding it for your own personal growth/learning. You tend to overlook anything, including facts, that are not seen as personally useful, and may disengage when not connected to the speaker as an individual or when not believing the speaker to be a knowledgeable resource. You may also tend to be influenced by strong personalities who appeal to emotions.
insights: Pay attention to what is being said rather than who is saying it.
When taking notes, you might try separating out “My Interests” from “Their Interests.” Then analyze what the data could mean for you and others.
Explain that you are taking notes to share the information with others.
Ask detailed questions to bring yourself into the sphere of the problem/issue. You might even develop questions beforehand to be prepared.
Engage throughout a presentation to demonstrate that you’re thinking and paying attention.

CV.atBest: Because of your mutually relational nature, you will tend to get along easily, each listening to the other with interest. You will also bring a depth of thought to the interaction that will be appreciated.
CV.challenges: While you enjoy connection, you may get bored and tune out when Connective Listeners focus on people rather than strategic issues. Alternatively, you may simply keep your real feelings hidden in order to maintain a positive relationship.
CV.suggestions: Watch out for any tendency to avoid addressing diﬃcult facts in order to maintain ease in the relationship. Ground your conversations in facts, data, or knowledge from previous experiences.

RV.atBest: You can appreciate the knowledge and experience Reflective Listeners bring to interactions while understanding their need to process information internally. You offer a welcoming space for Reflective Listeners to share their thoughts and consider ways their ideas may affect others.
RV.challenges: You may unwittingly expect Reflective Listeners to be just as open as you are, and therefore put pressure on them to speak before they’ve had a chance to process internally. While you may connect well, you may tend to overly focus on personal knowledge at the expense of other important facts, procedural considerations, and ideas.
RV.suggestions: Give Reflective Listeners plenty of time and room to process their thinking. When problem-solving together, ask yourself what other facts or ideas might be important to consider beyond the personal knowledge you both already bring.

AL.atBest: Analytical Listeners can help round out the “relational” angle you offer by contributing facts, data, and details. You can help translate the Analytical Listener’s literal approach into more humanistic terms when needed.
AL.challenges: You may get turned off by their literal thinking when it lacks a human or experiential component. They may see you as too concerned with others’ feelings or experience and may probe your ideas to see if they hold up under scrutiny.
AL.suggestions: Try not to take the Analytical Listener’s probing as a statement of personal mistrust. Rather, value them as a helpful “devil’s advocate” that can sharpen your thinking. Although you tend to speak from your feelings and experience, back up your ideas with facts and data as much as possible to get Analytical Listeners on the same page.

CL.atBest: You’ll tend to offer Conceptual Listeners room to be themselves and ideate, while making sure their ideas are taking all possible constituencies into consideration. Conceptual Listeners, in turn, can help take your own ideas to imaginative levels you hadn’t considered.
CL.challenges: Together, you may share a good “brainstorming” rapport but it may need some grounding in facts, details, and practicality. Alternatively, you may find a tension growing when their interest in ideas and possibilities conflicts with your interest in people and their present experiences.
CL.suggestions: Try to bring facts and data into your conversations as much as possible to prevent drifting into the clouds. Set a clear agenda to help guide your thinking and stay on track.

name: The Resolver
intro: As a RESOLVER, you rely on Connective Listening in combination with Reflective Listening and Analytical Listening. You listen for details that can be used to help solve issues that affect people. You will tend to tune out when options are presented without a plan to toward implementation.
shortDescription: Resolvers rely on Connective Listening in combination with Reflective Listening and Analytical Listening. They listen for details that can be used to help solve issues that affect people. They will tend to tune out when options are presented without a plan to toward implementation.
strengths: With a high preference for Connective Listening, you show concern for people and their feelings. You want to develop answers that will make a difference to others, so you delve into your own knowledgebase, network, and experience to provide well-evidenced solutions. You go deep to resolve whatever problems your team, company, or constituents are facing. You take facts and data into consideration when making decisions, and think about their implications for yourself and others. You are not prone to impulsivity; you deliberate before moving ahead.
possibleChallenges: You may be overly sensitive to the needs of others, which could reduce objectivity in decision- making. You may also surround yourself with like-minded individuals who don’t question your decisions. With your low preference for Conceptual Listening, you may underestimate the value that new, creative ideas can offer. You often ignore abstract thinkers who don’t provide sufficient, clear details to support their ideas. Finally, because you want to find the very best solution(s) for the people relying on you, you may tend to deliberate longer than necessary.
insights: Invite new and different people to help consider decisions, especially about chronic problems.
Hold facilitated brainstorming sessions to develop new thinking.
Create decision-making criteria ahead of time to prevent yourself from being swayed by authorities who make their case with self-selected logic.
Balance paying attention to both emotions and facts.
Ask others to help you decide when you’ve gathered enough data to make a decision and move ahead.

CV.atBest: Due to your mutually relational nature, you will tend to get along easily, each listening to the other with interest. You will personally bring nuanced knowledge and practical thinking to interactions that other Connective Listeners will appreciate.
CV.challenges: While you enjoy connecting, you may tune out Connective Listeners who don’t offer helpful information toward a resolution. You and like-minded Connective Listeners can be tempted to move forward toward solutions before considering alternative new, creative ideas.
CV.suggestions: Watch out for any tendency to make allies out of Connective Listeners who simply don’t push back on your reasoning. Try to welcome new ideas and abstract thinking into your decision-making with Connective Listeners.

RV.atBest: You can benefit from the knowledge and experience Reflective Listeners bring to interactions while naturally giving them space to process information internally. You can welcome Reflective Listeners’ contributions into any process, and also help them apply their knowledge to resolve current issues.
RV.challenges: You may slow each other down with your mutual interest in exploring all the angles before moving forward. You may be prone to favor Reflective Listeners who don’t question your decisions.
RV.suggestions: Maintain a clear agenda and structured time-frame when ideating with Reflective Listeners. Before moving forward with a decision, ask Reflective Listeners to help you think of ways your ideas might not work.

AL.atBest: You’ll tend to appreciate the Analytical Listener’s ability to vet your ideas and help filter out anything impractical. You can help take an Analytical Listener’s conclusions and apply them toward existing issues.
AL.challenges: You will get turned off when the Analytical Listener slows down progress by questioning more than seems necessary. Analytical Listeners may get frustrated if they feel you pushing them to move toward resolution for the sake of “getting it done” rather than “getting it right.”
AL.suggestions: Welcome the Analytical Listener’s probing as a helpful “devil’s advocate” that can sharpen your thinking. Back up your ideas with facts and data as much as possible when speaking with Analytical Listeners to help them understand your thinking.

CL.atBest: You’ll appreciate the ideation of Conceptual Listeners while making sure their ideas are taking all possible constituencies into consideration. Conceptual Listeners can help bolster your own thinking with a fresh sense of ideas and possibilities.
CL.challenges: Because you like to move steadily and methodically toward solutions, you may prematurely tune out Conceptual Listeners who are offering new, creative possibilities. Conceptual Listeners may find you unimaginative and unopen to new ways of thinking.
CL.suggestions: Watch for any tendencies to nod your head and “appease” Conceptual Listeners without taking them seriously. Welcome Conceptual Listeners as partners who can add new ways of thinking you hadn’t considered.

name: The Mover
intro: As a MOVER, you rely on Connective Listening in combination with Reflective Listening and Conceptual Listening. You listen for opportunities to take ideas forward. You will tend to tune out when details bog down the process towards implementation.
shortDescription: Movers rely on Connective Listening in combination with Reflective Listening and Conceptual Listening. They listen for opportunities to take ideas forward. They will tend to tune out when details bog down the process towards implementation.
strengths: With your high preference for Connective Listening, you pay attention to feelings and are often considered empathetic. You listen for ideas that relate to both self and others, and create new ideas that have merit for others to use. When faced with challenging issues or decisions you think could lead down the wrong path, you look for contrasting information that can re-direct an individual as well as a group, and you can rally people to act when convinced an idea is a good one.
possibleChallenges: With your lower preference for Analytical Listening, you may overlook facts that can add value to decisions. You tend to have a bias for action without sufficient deliberation, and don’t like to be slowed down. You can be impatient, especially when there are a lot of details to digest. In some situations, you can also be swayed by who is providing the information, rather than by exactly what is being said, and may form allegiances with people who might not always be reliable sources of accurate data.
insights: Focus on the relevance of information as well as the reliability of who is providing it.
Ask a variety of knowledgeable people about their experience with similar situations. Try not to rely upon the same people for advice.
Note details, in writing if possible, and evaluate their usefulness in the decision-making process.
Prepare fact-based questions to broaden your understanding of issues.
Balance attention to feelings with facts when interacting with others.

CV.atBest: Due to your mutually relational nature, you will tend to get along easily, each listening to the other with interest. You will also bring nuanced knowledge and ideas to interactions that other Connective Listeners will appreciate.
CV.challenges: While you enjoy connecting with others, you may tune out if Connective Listeners don’t offer much depth of knowledge or interesting ideas to the interaction. You can be tempted to move forward together toward solutions before considering all the necessary facts and contingencies.
CV.suggestions: Watch out for any tendency to make allies out of Connective Listeners who simply don’t push back on your reasoning. Try to ground your decision-making with Connective Listeners in as many facts and practical considerations as possible.

RV.atBest: You can benefit from the knowledge and experience Reflective Listeners bring to interactions while naturally giving them space to process information internally. You can welcome Reflective Listeners’ contributions into any process, and also help them get their own inspirations moving forward.
RV.challenges: Because you like to move things forward, you may get impatient with Reflective Listeners who take extra time to deliberate internally before deciding whether to “get on board.” Alternatively, you may become overly swayed by Reflective Listeners who establish themselves as knowledgeable subject-matter experts.
RV.suggestions: Always give Reflective Listeners plenty of time and room to process their thinking. Before moving too quickly with your agenda, solicit a Reflective Listener’s input by asking them to speak to previous experiences that would be relevant to the current situation.

AL.atBest: Analytical Listeners can help round out your thinking by subjecting your ideas and inspirations to close analysis and grounding them in facts and data. You can help take an Analytical Listener’s conclusions and apply them in ways they might not have considered.
AL.challenges: You may get turned off by the Analytical Listener’s approach when it appears to be too literal without enough of a human, experiential, or imaginative component. They may see you as too concerned with forward progress and may probe your ideas to see if they hold up under scrutiny.
AL.suggestions: Try not to take the Analytical Listener’s probing as a statement of personal mistrust. Rather, value them as a helpful “devil’s advocate” that can sharpen your thinking. Back up your ideas with facts and data as much as possible when speaking with Analytical Listeners to help them understand your thinking.

CL.atBest: You’ll appreciate the ideation of Conceptual Listeners while making sure their ideas are taking all possible constituencies into consideration. Conceptual Listeners, in turn, can help bolster your own thinking with a fresh sense of ideas and possibilities.
CL.challenges: Together, you may share a good “brainstorming” rapport but it may also need grounding in facts, details, and a discerning eye for what’s practical given the situation and resources. You may grow tired of Conceptual Listeners who don’t adequately fold other people or constituencies into their thinking, or whose ideas contradict what you’ve already learned from your own experience.
CL.suggestions: Try to bring facts and data into your conversations as much as possible to prevent moving forward without adequate details. Set a clear agenda with defined goals to help guide your thinking and stay on track.

name: The Implementer
intro: As an IMPLEMENTER, you rely on Connective Listening in combination with Analytical Listening. You listen for the facts that will enable a solution to be acted upon. You will tend to tune out when there is not a well-supported clear path forward.
shortDescription: Implementers rely on Connective Listening in combination with Analytical Listening. They listen for the facts that will enable a solution to be acted upon. They will tend to tune out when there is not a well-supported clear path forward.
strengths: You are adept at connecting the knowledge and experience of others to problems and issues under consideration. You engage others while identifying the problem at hand and setting a clear direction for addressing it. You are a balanced listener, considering “who” will be impacted as well as “what” the impact could be. You think critically about the relevance of facts and data as they are presented, and discard overly personal or biased information if there is not a rational explanation behind it.
possibleChallenges: You may find yourself torn between an allegiance to both feelings and facts, especially when they lead in opposite directions. You can also get bogged down in “what is” rather than “what can be.” In an attempt to involve people, you may end up asking leading questions rather than listening without an agenda. You may filter out details if there isn’t a people component, and might grow impatient when a group is not moving toward a solution.
insights: Use body language, voice and eye contact to show others you are interested in them as well as to the problem at hand.
Do not allow your impatience to overtake deliberation.
Ask both fact and feeling questions.
Take detailed notes that capture your observations of the concerns and behaviors of the people involved in the situation.
Allow the process to unfold rather than rushing it along because you “know the answer."

CV.atBest: You will tend to get along easily, each listening to the other with interest and ease, due to your Connective Listening preference. You will bring practical insights and a solutions-oriented approach to the interaction that Connective Listeners will appreciate.
CV.challenges: You may feel torn between facts and feelings when dealing with Connective Listeners, especially because you have an allegiance to both, but Connective Listeners will try appealing to your emotions more. Alternatively, you may simply tune out Connective Listeners who don’t ground their reasoning in facts.
CV.suggestions: Watch for any tendency to appease Connective Listeners by appearing to agree with them while not taking them seriously. Before writing them off, ask Connective Listeners to explain how they arrived at their conclusions and listen with an open mind.

RV.atBest: Reflective Listeners will enrich the exchange with useful insights and ideas based on their knowledge and experience. You can offer a welcoming space for Reflective Listeners while actively mining their knowledge and experience to address the needs of the situation.
RV.challenges: Because you tend to move forward toward solutions, you may grow impatient with Reflective Listeners who take too long to process their internal thinking and “get on board.” Reflective Listeners may think you tend to move too quickly ahead without considering all the angles.
RV.suggestions: Always allow Reflective Listeners plenty of time to process their thinking before pressuring them to voice their ideas or move forward. Count on Reflective Listeners as important resources who may have quietly and carefully considered angles you hadn’t, especially when they have previous experience in the subject under consideration.

AL.atBest: You will tend to share a mutual respect for facts, data, and details, and your like-mindedness will tend to make you good work-partners. You can also help Analytical Listeners consider how their analyses and researched opinions will be received by others.
AL.challenges: Together, you may tend to get bogged down in details without keeping track of the big picture. As stated above, you may feel torn between facts and feelings and become frustrated with Analytical Listeners who aren’t considering a “people” component.
AL.suggestions: To curb your tendency to get “stuck in the weeds” together, set a clear agenda with timing and priorities when collaborating. If you feel yourself being pulled toward an approach that ignores a “people” component, champion its importance with relevant data, statistics, or anecdotes to help them understand your reasoning.

CL.atBest: You can appreciate the fresh ideas and possibilities offered by Conceptual Listeners while helping them move toward practical implementation. Conceptual Listeners can help take your ideas to imaginative levels you hadn’t considered.
CL.challenges: Even though you might appear to be listening, you may prematurely tune out Conceptual Listeners whose ideas seem impractical at first blush. Conceptual Listeners may get frustrated with your literal approach that, in their view, might not be incorporating enough imaginative thinking.
CL.suggestions: Watch out for any tendency to nod in agreement with Conceptual Listeners without taking them seriously and instead ask questions that can help them ground their thinking. Seek out Conceptual Listeners as partners who can add creative new dimensions to solutions you’re already considering.

name: The Developer
intro: As a DEVELOPER, you rely on Connective Listening in combination with Analytical Listening and Reflective Listening. You listen for solutions that will add value to people and the situations they are dealing with. You will tend to tune out when there are unsubstantiated opinions rather than well-analyzed solutions.
shortDescription: Developers rely on Connective Listening in combination with Analytical Listening and Reflective Listening. They listen for solutions that will add value to people and the situations they are dealing with. They will tend to tune out when there are unsubstantiated opinions rather than well-analyzed solutions.
strengths: With your high preference for both Connective and Analytical Listening, you pay equal attention to people and data. You also shift easily into Reflective Listening to consider how the information you receive applies to your own experience and knowledge. Likewise, you consider how information can be applied to other people and functions. While you have a high tendency toward Connective Listening, you’re not easily swayed by people who do not have facts to support their opinions. When making decisions, you listen to the knowledge and experience that others have to offer.
possibleChallenges: You may have a bias for action that can tend to show up as impatience, which could lead you to “tune out” before all options are on the table. You may also tend to overlook ideas and new approaches that could add value for the same reason. You can be skeptical when an argument is presented without sufficient details to support it. You may also exhibit trust in some people, but not others, creating potential toward forming cliques.
insights: Carve out time to ask for ideas.
Patiently allow people with ideas to fully explain them, even if they do not come forth in a linear manner.
Explain your internal thoughts so that others know what is on your mind.
Jot down questions and concerns that you can ask after an interaction has occurred rather than interrupting in the moment.
Probe for details with an open mind rather than grilling others to satisfy your needs.

CV.atBest: Because of your mutually relational nature, you and Connective Listeners will tend to get along easily, each listening to the other with interest. You will bring practical insights and an experienced, solutions-oriented approach to the interaction that other Connective Listeners will appreciate.
CV.challenges: While you tend to listen with an open mind, you may tune out Connective Listeners if they appeal to emotions and advocate approaches that aren’t suﬃciently grounded in supportive details. You may also tend to favor Connective Listeners who support your approach without challenging it.
CV.suggestions: Instead of tuning out Connective Listeners who aren’t providing enough facts, ask them to explain the reasoning behind their position so you can better understand it. Notice when your relationship with others may be influencing whether or not you accept their ideas or feedback.

RV.atBest: You’ll appreciate Reflective Listeners who enrich the exchange with useful insights and ideas based on their knowledge and experience. You can offer a welcoming space for Reflective Listeners to share their thoughts and help vet their ideas for practicality.
RV.challenges: Because you tend to have a bias toward action, you may grow impatient if Reflective Listeners take too long to process their internal thinking before “getting on board.” Reflective Listeners may think you tend to move too quickly ahead without considering all the angles.
RV.suggestions: Always allow Reflective Listeners plenty of time to process their thinking before pressuring them to move forward. Count on Reflective Listeners as important resources who may have quietly and carefully considered angles you hadn’t, especially when they have previous experience in the subject under consideration.

AL.atBest: You will tend to share a mutual respect for facts, data, and details, and you will tend to make good work- partners. You can also help Analytical Listeners consider how their analyses and researched opinions will be received by others.
AL.challenges: Together, you may tend to get bogged down in details without keeping track of the big picture. You also may feel torn between facts and feelings and become frustrated with Analytical Listeners who aren’t considering a “people” component.
AL.suggestions: To curb your tendency to get “stuck in the weeds,” set a clear agenda with timing and priorities when collaborating. If you feel yourself being pulled by the Analytical Listener toward an approach that ignores a “people” component, champion its importance with relevant data, statistics, or anecdotes to help them understand your reasoning.

CL.atBest: You can appreciate the fresh ideas and possibilities offered by Conceptual Listeners while helping them move toward practical implementation. Conceptual Listeners can help take your ideas to imaginative levels you hadn’t considered.
CL.challenges: Even though you’ll probably appear to be listening, you may tune out Conceptual Listeners whose ideas seem impractical at first blush. Conceptual Listeners may get frustrated with your literal approach that might not be incorporating enough imaginative thinking.
CL.suggestions: Watch out for any tendency to nod in agreement with Conceptual Listeners without taking them seriously and instead ask questions that can help them ground their thinking. Seek out Conceptual Listeners as partners who can add creative new dimensions to solutions you’re already considering.

name: The Practitioner
intro: As a PRACTITIONER, you rely on Connective Listening in combination with Analytical Listening and Conceptual Listening. You listen for fact-based ideas that can be used by many people in a variety of situations. You will tend to tune out when there is not an audience to try new approaches with.
shortDescription: Practitioners rely on Connective Listening in combination with Analytical Listening and Conceptual Listening. They listen for fact-based ideas that can be used by many people in a variety of situations. They will tend to tune out when there is not an audience to try new approaches with.
strengths: You balance listening for both feelings and facts. You are adept at idea-generation, and evaluate ideas for practicality from both a “people” and “process” point of view. Your ideas can appeal to both creative and logical thinkers. When you’ve decided on a direction or plan, you can persuade others to get behind it. You have an ear for what is most important and whom to pay attention to in any given area.
possibleChallenges: Your low preference for Reflective Listening may lead you to become co-opted by charismatic people; you probably consider your own opinion less than the opinions of others. You also might ignore previous experiences that can shed light on the current situation. Your proclivity for both analytical and conceptual thinking can create a conflict between leaping ahead in good faith and waiting for a logical path forward. You may have a difficult time identifying when there are sufficient options and/or facts already available to you, and therefore may ask for more information than is necessary to make a decision.
insights: Watch out for the tendency to ignore previous experiences that can shed light on the current situation.
Ask yourself, “What do I know from past experience that I can apply in this scenario?”
Notice you may become torn between leaping ahead in good faith and waiting for a logical path forward.
Realize you often do not consider your own opinions as much as the opinions of others.
Notice if you are asking for more information than is necessary to make a decision because you have a difficult time identifying when there are sufficient options and/or facts.

CV.atBest: Because you are both highly relational, you and Connective Listeners will tend to get along easily, each listening to the other with interest. You will bring practical ideas backed with a solutions-oriented approach that Connective Listeners will appreciate.
CV.challenges: While you tend to listen with an open mind, you may tune out Connective Listeners if they appeal to emotions or advocate approaches that seem impractical to you. You may be prone to form friendships with Connective Listeners who find your ideas convincing but don’t offer a practical check on them.
CV.suggestions: Instead of tuning out Connective Listeners who aren’t providing enough facts, ask them to explain the reasoning behind their position so you can better understand it. Be on guard for any tendency to form cliques with other Connective Listeners.

RV.atBest: You’ll appreciate Reflective Listeners who enrich the exchange with useful insights and ideas based on their knowledge and experience. You can offer a welcoming space for Reflective Listeners to share their thoughts and help vet their ideas for practicality.
RV.challenges: You may grow impatient if Reflective Listeners take too long to process their internal thinking before “getting on board.” Reflective Listeners may think you tend to move too quickly ahead without considering all the angles.
RV.suggestions: Always allow Reflective Listeners plenty of time to process their thinking before pressuring them to move forward. Count on Reflective Listeners as important resources who may have quietly and carefully considered angles you hadn’t, especially when they have previous experience in the subject under consideration.

AL.atBest: You will tend to share a mutual respect for facts, data, and details, and you will tend to make good work- partners. You can also help Analytical Listeners consider how their analyses and researched opinions will be received by others.
AL.challenges: You may get frustrated with and tune out Analytical Listeners who don’t seem to be considering a “people” component. Neither of you are likely to take into consideration any past experiences that could add value to the issue under consideration.
AL.suggestions: If you feel yourself being pulled by the Analytical Listener toward an approach that ignores a “people” component, champion its importance with relevant data, statistics, or anecdotes to help them understand your reasoning. Be sure to reflect on how past experiences could offer relevant insights to the current situation.

CL.atBest: You can appreciate the fresh ideas and possibilities offered by Conceptual Listeners while helping them move toward practical implementation. Conceptual Listeners can help take your ideas to imaginative levels you might not have considered.
CL.challenges: Even though you’ll probably appear to be listening, you may tune out Conceptual Listeners whose ideas seem impractical at first blush. Conceptual Listeners may get frustrated with your practical approach that, in their view, might not be incorporating enough imaginative thinking.
CL.suggestions: Watch out for any tendency to nod in agreement with Conceptual Listeners without taking them seriously and instead ask questions that can help them ground their thinking. Somewhat paradoxically, also watch out for the occasional charismatic Conceptual Listener who establishes themselves as an expert and may convince you to suspend your analytical thinking and incorporate their ideas without suﬃcient grounding.

name: The Collaborator
intro: As a COLLABORATOR, you rely on Connective Listening in combination with Conceptual Listening. You listen to find ways to collaborate with others about new ideas. You will tend to tune out when there is no one else to share ideas with.
shortDescription: Collaborators rely on Connective Listening in combination with Conceptual Listening. They listen to find ways to collaborate with others about new ideas. They will tend to tune out when there is no one else to share ideas with.
strengths: Collaborating with others comes naturally to you, and you can do it almost tirelessly. You are adept at drawing upon the knowledge and strength of everyone in the room when generating ideas, and you see opportunities where new concepts can be applied. You enjoy interacting with others, acknowledging both your own feelings and the feelings of others when they arise.
possibleChallenges: Your interactions may be more emotional or sentimental than fact-based. In fact, you may enjoy idea-generation with others so much that you engage in brainstorming for the social aspects over-and-above the practical task, and may not take enough time to evaluate ideas closely. While you are adept at generating ideas, you almost require a connection to the interest of others before this can happen, and may overlook how your own previous experience could add value to the current situation.
insights: Invite others to share their ideas to begin collaborating.
Create decision-making criteria ahead of time to keep the idea-generation on a practical track rather than letting it drift into more of a social opportunity.
Rather than chiming in constantly, consider which of your ideas you can write down to offer later.
Notice when your body language could be overpowering others, and temper your physical enthusiasm.
Ask qualifying questions such as, “What options have you tried that might work here? What ideas can we come up with to help our department reach its goal?”

CV.atBest: Due to your mutually relational natures, you will tend to get along easily, each listening to the other with interest. You will also bring fresh ideas to the interaction that the Connective Listener will appreciate.
CV.challenges: Together, you may enjoy brainstorming for the social aspects without paying enough attention to the facts and details required to move forward with practical solutions. You may wrongly assume that because of mutual enthusiasm and similarity in thinking, you must be on the right track.
CV.suggestions: Remember that mutual enthusiasm is not enough; try to ground your conversations in facts, data, and knowledge you each bring from previous experiences. Consider keeping an agenda that has a structure and clearly defined goals to curb the possibility of drifting into prolonged ideating.

RV.atBest: You will offer Reflective Listeners a welcoming space to contribute their thoughts, knowledge, and experience. Reflective Listeners, in turn, can absorb your ideas and offer back insights based on their own knowledge and expertise.
RV.challenges: Because you like feeling connected to others through idea-generation, you may become uncomfortable with Reflective Listeners who are slow to offer their contributions. Reflective Listeners may tune out when the pace of your brainstorming doesn’t allow enough space to deeply consider all the angles.
RV.suggestions: Always give Reflective Listeners plenty of time and room to process new information before having to offer back their input. When you need to solicit their input, try asking about previous experiences they’ve had relevant to the issue under consideration.

AL.atBest: Analytical Listeners can help vet your ideas based on existing available resources and move your inspirations toward practical implementation. You, in turn, can offer a sense of imagination and fresh direction to balance their “literalness”, especially when they become stuck in details.
AL.challenges: You may tune out Analytical Listeners when they fail to incorporate either an imaginative or human component into their solutions. Analytical Listeners might stop listening seriously to your ideation—or begin to ask probing questions— when you don’t offer enough practical rationale to back up your thinking.
AL.suggestions: Try to value the Analyzing Listener’s probing questions as a helpful grounding mechanism to ensure your ideas are thoroughly vetted. While you habitually tend to speak from ideas and emotions, try to incorporate more facts and data when speaking with Analytical Listeners. If you do, they will be more likely to see the importance of your thinking.

CL.atBest: You will naturally enjoy brainstorming together and will likely feel that you’re on the same wavelength. You will make sure those outside the immediate conversation—the rest of the team, the company, and other constituents—are included within the scope of your brainstorming.
CL.challenges: If you aren’t careful, you can stay in continuous “ideating” mode without arriving at practical solutions. In your naturally comfortable brainstorming rapport, you may reinforce each other’s excitement about new possible directions while mutually ignoring the feasibility of their implementation.
CL.suggestions: When brainstorming, provide clearly defined priorities and outcomes which can serve as a framework within which to better focus thinking & ideation. While you'll naturally speak of ideas and how they might impact people, try also to round out your brainstorming with facts, data, and lessons from previous experiences wherever possible.

name: The Caretaker
intro: As a CARETAKER, you rely on Connective Listening in combination with Conceptual Listening and Reflective Listening. You listen for ways to combine existing knowledge with new ideas that will serve others. You will tend to tune out when there is not a human element to consider.
shortDescription: Caretakers rely on Connective Listening in combination with Conceptual Listening and Reflective Listening. They listen for ways to combine existing knowledge with new ideas that will serve others. They will tend to tune out when there is not a human element to consider.
strengths: You are versatile in listening to a wide range of people. You naturally provide options to handle situations that may affect others, and balance your own self-interests with others’ needs. As an original thinker, you generate ideas that others have not previously considered, while enthusiastically supporting the input of those around you.
possibleChallenges: With a low preference for Analytical Listening, you may tend to overlook data that may be necessary for making decisions, and grow bored when presented with what seem like endless details. You are much more likely to be led by feelings rather than facts. You can be swayed by who is speaking rather than what is being said, and might be prone to form cliques of like-minded people.
insights: Ask for details and reflect on them before moving to a solution.
Listen critically to information to evaluate its value and importance.
Balance what is being said with who is saying it.
Take notes to check the efficacy of your ideas.
Bring in new people and be open to ideas that are different from yours.

CV.atBest: Due to your mutually relational natures, you will tend to get along easily, each listening to the other with interest. You will also bring fresh ideas to the interaction and speak from experience that the Connective Listener will appreciate.
CV.challenges: Together, you may enjoy brainstorming for the feeling of connection without paying enough attention to the facts and details required to move forward with practical solutions. You may be swayed by each other and prematurely assume that because of mutual enthusiasm and similarity in thinking, you must be on the right track.
CV.suggestions: Remember that mutual enthusiasm is not enough; try to ground your conversations in facts, data, and knowledge you each bring from previous experiences. Try to keep an agenda to the conversation that has a structure and clearly defined goals to curb the possibility of drifting into prolonged ideating.

RV.atBest: You will tend to offer a welcoming atmosphere for Reflective Listeners and enthusiastically support their input. Reflective Listeners can bolster your own ideas with helpful insights from their knowledge and expertise.
RV.challenges: The Reflective Listener may be less interested in “connecting” than you are, and may tune out when your social side starts distracting from the task at hand. You might be easily swayed by Reflective Listeners who show impressive depth and expertise in a given field or subject.
RV.suggestions: When collaborating, provide yourselves with clearly defined priorities and outcomes and make an effort to support your mutual thinking with relevant facts and details. If you find yourself too often deferring to the Reflective Listener’s expertise, remember to pause and tap into your own depths of knowledge.

AL.atBest: Analytical Listeners can help vet your ideas based on available resources, time or budget, and move your inspirations toward practical implementation. You can offer a sense of imagination and possible direction to balance the Analyzing Listener’s “literalness”, especially when they become stuck in details.
AL.challenges: You may tune out Analytical Listeners when they fail to incorporate either an imaginative or human component into their solutions. Analytical Listeners may stop listening seriously to your ideation—or may begin to ask probing questions— when you don’t offer enough practical rationale to back up your thinking.
AL.suggestions: Try to value the Analyzing Listener’s probing questions as a helpful grounding mechanism to ensure your ideas are thoroughly vetted. While you habitually tend to speak from ideas and emotions, try to incorporate more facts and data when speaking to Analytical Listeners. If you do, they will be more likely to see the importance of your thinking.

CL.atBest: You and Conceptual Listeners will naturally enjoy brainstorming together and will likely feel that you’re on the same wavelength. You will also help ground the ideas in consideration for others while drawing upon what has and hasn’t worked in the past.
CL.challenges: If you aren’t careful, you can stay in continuous “ideating” mode without arriving at practical solutions. In your naturally comfortable brainstorming rapport, you may reinforce each other’s excitement about new possible directions while ignoring relevant facts, data, and resource considerations.
CL.suggestions: When brainstorming, provide yourselves with clearly defined priorities and outcomes which can serve as a framework within which to better focus thinking & ideation. While you'll naturally speak of new ideas and how they might positively impact people, try to round out your brainstorming by intentionally looking for problem areas—such as conflicting facts or resource considerations—that may help ground your thinking.

name: The Experimenter
intro: As an EXPERIMENTER, you rely on Connective Listening in combination with Conceptual Listening and Analytical Listening. You listen for ideas that can be tried on new and chronic problems across the organization. You will tend to tune out when there is too much deliberation and reluctance to see what might work.
shortDescription: Experimenters rely on Connective Listening in combination with Conceptual Listening and Analytical Listening. They listen for ideas that can be tried on new and chronic problems across the organization. They will tend to tune out when there is too much deliberation and reluctance to see what might work.
strengths: You are interested in understanding how situations affect people, and are not afraid to experiment with new ideas that can bring about systemic improvements. You trust in the knowledge and experience of others, and are open to new ideas as long as there is supporting, detailed documentation. During information gathering or brainstorming, you naturally seek to uncover both facts and feelings that underlie the issues you are tasked to examine; thus you collaborate well with both abstract and logical thinkers.
possibleChallenges: With a low preference for Reflective Listening, you might overlook how your own knowledge and experience could add value to the situation at hand. With your high Connective and Conceptual preferences, you may be tempted to move into action without sufficient deliberation, leading to costly errors. Your receptivity to Analytical Listening could help mitigate this, but it is likely you will look at evidence through the eyes of your trusted relationships, leading to unintended biases that influence what you take in and from whom.
insights: Examine what you already know about a situation to make your experience relevant to the issue at hand.
Develop criteria to evaluate information provided rather than accepting the input because it comes from someone you trust.
Invite both creative and rational thinkers into brainstorming sessions.
Ask open, non-judgmental questions to assess the readiness to move forward.
Enroll people whose work will be involved and/or affected to ensure their engagement.

CV.atBest: Because you are both highly relational, you will tend to get along easily, each listening to the other with interest. You will also bring fresh ideas to the interaction and incorporate facts and details that the Connective Listener will appreciate.
CV.challenges: Together, you may enjoy brainstorming for the feeling of connection and forward movement without grounding the conversation enough in your personal wisdom gained from past experience. You may be convincing to Connective Listeners and prematurely assume that because of mutual enthusiasm and agreement in thinking, you must be on the right track.
CV.suggestions: Bring a clear set of goals to your brainstorming that lists what you need to know before making any final decisions; resist the temptation to move into action too quickly. Make an effort to look inward from time-to-time when brainstorming to see how your own knowledge and experience could add to the situation.

RV.atBest: A Reflective Listener will be a grounding partner to your thinking with helpful insights based on their knowledge and expertise. You, in turn, can help reflective listeners test out and apply their insights in many different ways.
RV.challenges: You may be tempted to move quickly into action and thus ignore Reflective Listeners who want more time to study / think about the situation. Reflective listeners, in turn, may become frustrated with your tendency to move ahead without suﬃcient deliberation.
RV.suggestions: Always give Reflective Listeners plenty of time to process incoming information before asking them to offer their input. When starting to brainstorm, ask Reflective Listeners if they've had previous experiences relevant to the topic at hand as a way to garner their early participation.

AL.atBest: You will appreciate the Analytical Listener’s ability to add concrete data to inform your decisions and inspirations. They, in turn, will appreciate the numerous ways you think to apply the information they’ve offered.
AL.challenges: You may tune out Analytical Listeners when they fail to incorporate either an imaginative or human component into their solutions. They may question your forward movement, especially if you’ve only considered data from a few, trusted sources.
AL.suggestions: Welcome an Analytical Listener’s critical questions, especially if you find yourself moving forward on limited information. To help Analytical Listeners understand the value of your thinking, communicate your ideas and inspirations using as much factual and data-driven information as possible.

CL.atBest: You and Conceptual Listeners will naturally enjoy brainstorming together and will likely feel that you’re on the same wavelength. You will make sure those outside the immediate conversation—the rest of the team, the company, and other constituents—are included within the scope of your brainstorming.
CL.challenges: You may prematurely assume that because of your natural brainstorming rapport, you’re automatically on the right track. If you aren’t careful, you can stay in continuous “ideating” mode and forget to tap into your own relevant knowledge based on past experience.
CL.suggestions: When brainstorming, provide yourselves with clearly defined priorities and outcomes which can serve as a framework within which to better focus thinking & ideation. While you'll naturally incorporate available data with new ideas to see how they might positively impact people, try also to round out your brainstorming by intentionally looking for ways your previous lessons and experiences could add value. REFLECTIVE LISTENING PROFILES

name: The Reflector
intro: As a REFLECTOR, you rely on Reflective Listening most. You listen to connect information to previous experiences as well as what you know and care about. You will tend to tune out when there is not sufficient relevance to your own experience or expertise.
shortDescription: Reflectors rely on Reflective Listening most. They listen to connect information to previous experiences as well as what they know and care about. They will tend to tune out when there is not sufficient relevance to their own experience or expertise.
strengths: You relate information to your own knowledge of situations to give it more personal meaning. As a result, you tend to think about what is being said before sharing anything with others. You tend to be grounded in your decision-making. You are not swayed by overly enthusiastic thinking or appeals to emotions, nor do you require endless details when assessing the efficacy of information. Because you value your privacy, you respect the privacy of others.
possibleChallenges: Since you relate incoming information back to your own mental “encyclopedia” of experience and knowledge, you can be overly introspective and slow to share thoughts and feelings. Another side- effect of this reference to your own encyclopedia is you sometimes ignore facts and data that could otherwise influence decisions. Because you value personal space, you can be prone to working alone even when collaboration is called for, and may overlook how your own knowledge and experience can be useful for others. As a result, you can easily be misinterpreted as disengaged or disinterested.
insights: Interact early in the communication process to demonstrate your attention.
Some reflectors tend to hold back. If this describes you, make sure to share what you are thinking and what you know.
Try asking questions that mirror the speaking style of the person(s) speaking.
Use body language, voice, and eye contact to show engagement.
Ask yourself how you might be able to apply the information you are receiving to an audience broader than your own interests.

CV.atBest: Connective Listeners will be interested to hear your well-reasoned thoughts and ideas to see how they can further contribute to the situation. While you bring deep thinking to the table, Connective Listeners can demonstrate how your thoughts might affect other relevant people, customers, or processes in ways you hadn’t considered.
CV.challenges: Because you don’t tend to speak unless your thinking is fully fleshed out, Connective Listeners may become uncomfortable with your silence. Since Connective Listeners are poised to act, and you take time to consider, you may get frustrated when they want you to connect or share before you’ve had a chance to process your thoughts and opinions.
CV.suggestions: Try not to be annoyed by Connective Listeners who solicit your thoughts or try to make connections— they are genuinely interested in the knowledge you have to share. When you’re not ready to speak, don’t hesitate to let Connective Listeners know you need more time to consider the issue at hand. They’ll respect this.

RV.atBest: You share a mutual respect for knowledge and understanding, and will tend to appreciate each other’s areas of expertise. You will both understand each other’s tendency to ruminate and confirm your own thoughts or opinions before speaking.
RV.challenges: You may be tempted to spend too much time probing areas of expertise together (in either a friendly or competitive way) rather than moving forward with the task-at-hand. You each could assume that because of similarity in thinking you’re on the right track.
RV.suggestions: Before you get too far, ask how your ideas might impact others, and what additional facts or data you would need to move forward. To help diffuse any unnecessarily competitive feelings, show that you appreciate their knowledge or expertise when they offer important insights.

AL.atBest: Analytical Listeners will tend to appreciate the personal knowledge and thoughtful perspectives you bring to interactions. You, in turn, will tend to appreciate their logical approach and will trust them to vet your ideas for practicality.
AL.challenges: Analytical Listeners may grow impatient and ask probing questions when you’re overly thoughtful or slow to formulate your ideas. You, in turn, may get turned off when their responses seem too quick and literal-minded to have considered the full depth and scope of the situation.
AL.suggestions: Try not to take their probing as a statement of personal mistrust. Rather, value them as a helpful “devil’s advocate” that can support your thinking. Although you tend to speak from your own knowledge, back up your ideas with facts and data as much as possible to help them see the value of your thinking.

CL.atBest: Conceptual Listeners will offer fresh ideas and possibilities you may not have thought of on your own. You, in turn, can offer a practical check on their ideation based on your past knowledge and experience.
CL.challenges: You may get frustrated by a Conceptual Listener’s ability to ideate indefinitely as they toss forward half- formed thoughts to see where they might go. Conceptual Listeners may grow frustrated with your tendency to “hold back” and not speak until you’ve fully considered everything.
CL.suggestions: Since Conceptual Listeners love to brainstorm, consider using your interactions with them as an opportunity to practice speaking more spontaneously. Don’t be too quick to disregard Conceptual Listeners' ideas. Ask questions about how the ideas will work in order to understand their thinking and share any previous experiences that might support or validate their vision.

name: The Empathizer
intro: As an EMPATHIZER, you rely on Reflective Listening in combination with Connective Listening. You listen for information that can impact people. You will tend to tune out when there is not a human component to consider.
shortDescription: Empathizers rely on Reflective Listening in combination with Connective Listening. They listen for information that can impact people. They will tend to tune out when there is not a human component to consider.
strengths: Your preference for both Reflective and Connective Listening suggests you care about employees as people and not just workers. When presented with new information, you focus on its meaning for yourself as well as others, and naturally filter out details that aren’t relevant. You collaborate easily with individuals and small groups. When you find information personally relevant, you naturally consider who else may benefit from it.
possibleChallenges: While Reflective and Connective Listening usually provide a good balance to each other, in some circumstances they may lead to an internal tug-of-war, especially when listening for yourself or for others would lead to conflicting results. You are often expected to translate information for others to use, which can be tedious. You might easily overlook details and information that do not have a personal element. You may disengage when not stimulated by the speaker or the information, and with your relatively low preference for Analytical Listening, you may be easily swayed and quick to trust people who can be persuasive.
insights: Pay attention to what is being said as well as who is saying it.
Take notes that both capture meaning of information for you and how the information can be applied to others.
Ask detailed questions to gain an understanding of the problem/issue.
Engage throughout a presentation to demonstrate that you are thinking and paying attention.
Think critically and assess the input of charismatic people for validity rather than being swayed by your interest in them.

CV.atBest: Because you are both highly relational, you and Connective Listeners will likely share a strong rapport and get along easily. Connective Listeners will appreciate the insights and considerations you bring to interactions.
CV.challenges: Your mutual interest in connecting on a personal level may sometimes distract from your focus on the task-at-hand. You may have a tendency to “go along with” Connective Listeners and avoid raising doubts openly, even when you disagree with their thinking.
CV.suggestions: Because you’ll both tend to be quite relational, when working together, provide yourselves with clearly defined priorities and outcomes which can serve as a framework within which to better focus thinking & ideation. If you find yourself doubting a Connective Listener’s thinking but outwardly agreeing to maintain ease in the relationship, speak up and at least ask for more time to think on the situation.

RV.atBest: You will tend to work well with other Reflective Listeners, appreciating the knowledge and experience they bring to interactions while relating to their need to process information internally. While you’ll share a mutual appreciation for each other’s knowledge and experience, you can also help Reflective Listeners identify new ways their insights or ideas may benefit others.
RV.challenges: While you and Reflective Listeners may connect well you may overly focus on personal knowledge at the expense of other important facts, procedural considerations, and ideas. You may tend to assume that because of similarity in thinking, you’re therefore on the right track.
RV.suggestions: When brainstorming, provide yourselves with clearly defined priorities and timing goals to keep yourselves moving forward with the task at hand. Actively seek available facts and data to give concrete support to your ideas and conclusions.

AL.atBest: You will tend to appreciate the Analytical Listener’s ability to ground discussions in relevant facts, data, and practical considerations. They will appreciate your ability to champion the importance of their literal fact-finding approach to the rest of the team when needed.
AL.challenges: You may be tempted to tune out Analytical Listeners when their approach appears to be too literal and detail-oriented without enough of a human or experiential component. They may see you as too concerned with people’s feelings or personal experience and probe your own ideas to see if they hold up under scrutiny.
AL.suggestions: Try not to take the Analytical Listener’s probing as a statement of personal mistrust. Rather, value them as a helpful “devil’s advocate” that can sharpen your thinking. Although you tend to speak from your feelings and experience, back up your ideas with facts and data as much as possible to help them understand the value of your thinking.

CL.atBest: You’ll tend to offer Conceptual Listeners room to be themselves and ideate, while offering your own knowledgeable input and making sure their ideas are taking all relevant constituencies into consideration. Conceptual Listeners can help take your own ideas to imaginative levels you hadn’t considered.
CL.challenges: When “brainstorming” remember to ground information with facts, details, and practical resource considerations. You may tune out when the Conceptual Listener’s interest in ideas and possibilities conflicts with your interest in present knowledge and its tangible application for others’ benefit.
CL.suggestions: Rather than tune out or resist what you see as unrealistic or irrelevant, ask Conceptual Listeners exactly how they imagine their ideas will benefit the situation for others. When brainstorming together, ground your thinking in as many relevant facts, data, and details as you can.

name: The Integrator
intro: As an INTEGRATOR, you rely on Reflective Listening in combination with Connective Listening and Analytical Listening. You listen for knowledge that can be combined to find the right answers. You will tend to tune out when the people affected by a decision are not considered.
shortDescription: Integrators rely on Reflective Listening in combination with Connective Listening and Analytical Listening. They listen for knowledge that can be combined to find the right answers. They will tend to tune out when the people affected by a decision are not considered.
strengths: With a high preference for Reflective, Connective, and Analytical Listening, you will focus relatively equally on personal relevance, other people, and problem solving. In conversations, you will attend to both feelings and facts, while turning a critical, evaluative ear to the information being presented to see how it matches up with your own knowledge. You seek practical uses for information under consideration, and won’t lose sight of a decision’s impact on other colleagues, departments, or clients. You value the experience and knowledge that others bring, but prefer those with a factual basis, and won’t be swayed by unfounded opinions.
possibleChallenges: Since you are considerate of both facts and people, you’re likely pulled between your head and heart when making decisions. You may tend to be swayed by people with compelling logical arguments more than those with grand ideas. With your low preference for Conceptual Listening, you may ignore creative ideas before giving them proper consideration. You like to get to the point and move forward on decisions, and therefore may get impatient when you feel a lack of progression.
insights: Be clear about what you want from an interaction to avoid jumping from one mode of listening to another.
Carve out time to develop options and new ideas.
Create questions that satisfy your need for both feelings and facts.
Allow the big picture to be addressed without pressing right away for details.
Develop criteria that will guide your decisions and draw up a stakeholder map to identify everyone involved and affected by the situation.

CV.challenges: • You may tune out when Connective Listeners try to stay on a personal level and fail to offer much depth of knowledge or helpful information relevant to the task at hand. When seeking solutions together, you may be tempted to move forward into action before relevant new creative ideas or possibilities have had a chance to surface.
CV.suggestions: • Watch for any tendency to make allies out of Connective Listeners who are overly convinced by your reasoning and don’t provide much push-back. • Try to welcome new ideas and abstract thinking into your decision-making with Connective Listeners.

RV.challenges: • While you tend to respect the Reflective Listener’s need to mull over information, you may grow impatient if this conflicts with your desire to move forward. • Reflective Listeners, in turn, may tune out if they perceive you moving forward without enough deliberation.
RV.suggestions: • Try to always allow Reflective Listeners time and space to process their thinking and consider all the information in their heads before offering feedback. • Ask Reflective Listeners about previous experiences they’ve had relevant to the current situation as a way to pull them in and solicit their input.

AL.challenges: • You may have a tendency to be occasionally swayed by Analytical Listeners who offer compelling logical arguments. • Alternatively, you can also get tired of and tune out Analytical Listeners whose approach is “too literal” and lacks a human or experiential component.

CL.challenges: • Because you like to move steadily forward on a factual basis, you may tune out, thinking their ideation on possibilities is too unrealistic at first glance. • Conceptual Listeners may tune you out when you appear unimaginative and not open to new ways of thinking.
CL.suggestions: • Before tuning out Conceptual Listeners, try redirecting their thinking to a factual basis by asking how they imagine their ideas being implemented. • Welcome Conceptual Listeners as important team members who can help fill in your blind spots with new ways of thinking you hadn’t considered.

name: The Engager
intro: As an ENGAGER, you rely on Reflective Listening in combination with Connective Listening and Conceptual Listening. You listen for ideas that will draw in people. You will tend to tune out when inundated with facts or data that seem boring instead of hearing options that others might appreciate.
shortDescription: Engagers rely on Reflective Listening in combination with Connective Listening and Conceptual Listening. They listen for ideas that will draw in people. They will tend to tune out when inundated with facts or data that seem boring instead of hearing options that others might appreciate.
strengths: With a high preference for Reflective Listening, you consider the relevance of information to your own self-interests, knowledge, and experience. However, you also listen “Connectively” and consider the relevance of information to colleagues, managers, employees, and clients. In short, you focus on your own feelings as well as the feelings of others. You also enjoy generating ideas and expanding the possible applications of information being considered. You enjoy brainstorming and collaborate well with others to find new options.
possibleChallenges: With a low preference for Analytical Listening, you may get co-opted by feelings and overlook facts. You will pay attention to who is sharing the information more than what the information is about. You tend to let other people debate the details or may just ignore them entirely, leading you to make decisions without considering all the available data yourself. You might also tend to establish alliances of trusted people who share similar ideas but may not always be the best resources for diverse thinking.
insights: Develop lists of detailed, fact-driven questions before engaging in a listening situation.
Ask yourself, “What can I learn from this information and what might I share from what I already know?”
Space your questions throughout an interaction, rather than all at once, which will provide people a chance to think.
When selecting the people who will work on an issue, carefully assess their appropriateness for the situation.
Take notes during the interaction under two headings: FACTS and IDEAS. Try to capture as many facts as you do ideas from what is being said.

CV.atBest: Connective Listeners will appreciate your willingness to engage conversations, offer insights, and consider new ideas. You will appreciate the Connective Listener’s welcoming attitude and ear for how situations will affect the constituencies involved.
CV.challenges: You may tune out when Connective Listeners try to stay on a personal level and fail to offer much depth of knowledge or helpful new ideas. When seeking solutions together, you could be “swayed” by each other without a strong foundation in facts and data.
CV.suggestions: Watch for any tendency to make allies out of Connective Listeners who are easily convinced by your ideas and don’t provide much push-back. Try to ground your mutual brainstorming in as much available data as possible, since fact-finding won’t be either of your first impulses.

RV.atBest: You will appreciate the knowledge and experience Reflective Listeners bring to interactions while understanding their need to process information internally. You can also support Reflective Listeners’ thinking with new ideas and possibilities that take other constituencies into further consideration.
RV.challenges: While you tend to respect the Reflective Listener’s need to mull over information, you may grow impatient if this conflicts with your desire to engage and ideate. You might be easily swayed or convinced by Reflective Listeners who present themselves as subject matter experts and decline to challenge their thinking if necessary.
RV.suggestions: If you need them to engage more, ask them about previous experiences they’ve had relevant to the current situation as a way to pull them in and solicit their input. If you find yourself “going along with” Reflective Listeners who seem to know what they’re talking about, make an effort to double-check the facts for yourself.

AL.atBest: Analytical Listeners will help you vet your ideas and filter out anything impractical. You, in turn, can help take an Analytical Listener’s conclusions and apply them in ways they might not have considered.
AL.challenges: Because you tend to appreciate feelings and the human dimension over strict facts, you may become bored and tune out Analytical Listeners who seem literal, dry, and colorless. They may see you as too concerned with people’s feelings or personal experience and may probe your own ideas to see if they hold up under scrutiny.
AL.suggestions: Try not to take the Analytical Listener’s probing as a statement of personal mistrust. Rather, value them as a helpful “devil’s advocate” that can sharpen your thinking. Although you tend to speak from your feelings and experience, back up your ideas with facts and data as much as possible when speaking with Analytical Listeners to help them understand the value of your thinking.

CL.atBest: You will tend to share a strong rapport based on your mutual interest in brainstorming new ideas and possibilities. You can also support Reflective Listeners’ thinking with new ideas and possibilities that take other constituencies into further consideration.
CL.challenges: Your mutual love for ideating could become a weakness when time is of the essence and you’re unable to land at practical solutions. You may also form close alliances with Conceptual Listeners who share similar thinking but don’t challenge your ideas to help them become better.
CL.suggestions: When brainstorming, provide yourselves with clearly defined priorities and outcomes which can serve as a framework within which to better focus thinking and ideation. Don’t assume you have a good idea just because you both agree it’s good; ground your thinking in as many relevant facts, data, and details as you can.

name: The Judge
intro: As a JUDGE, you rely on Reflective Listening in combination with Analytical Listening. You listen to assess the efficacy of the information provided. You will tend to tune out if there are not trusted facts to substantiate the information.
shortDescription: Judges rely on Reflective Listening in combination with Analytical Listening. They listen to assess the efficacy of the information provided. They will tend to tune out if there are not trusted facts to substantiate the information.
strengths: With a high preference for Reflective and Analytical Listening, you apply personal knowledge and experience in a practical way to any issue under consideration. You think critically about the relevance of the facts and connect them to what you already know. You are not persuaded by charismatic people or calls to emotion, and you discard overly personal or biased information. You stick to the facts and focus on the details rather than who is presenting them.
possibleChallenges: With your relatively low preference for Connective Listening, you may overlook feelings behind information and may sometimes be perceived as cold. You can get bogged down in “what is” at the expense of what “could be,” and tend to require certainty before committing to a decision. You likely enjoy working alone, and may stay inside your head to solve a problem without sharing your thoughts with others. Because you prize thorough, critical thinking, you may grow impatient when others “throw around” half-formed ideas in brainstorming sessions.
insights: You can be hard to read and give an impression of disinterest when you are actually thinking. Use body language, voice and eye contact to show others you are connecting.
Invite creative thinkers to meetings to stimulate fresh thinking and offset your analytical tendencies.
Ask both fact and feeling questions. You may have to prepare the “feeling” questions beforehand to be comfortable using them.
Take notes and share them.
Allow the process to unfold by inviting others to engage rather than rushing it along because you already “know-the-answer.”

CV.atBest: You can take a Connective Listener’s ideas or inspirations and let them know exactly what will work and what won’t. Connective Listeners, in turn, will appreciate your straightforward and practical style, ensure your value is understood by others, and explore ways your conclusions can benefit wider constituencies.
CV.challenges: Because you stick to facts and focus on details, you may tune out Connective Listeners who seem too relational or biased in their thinking. Connective Listeners, in turn, may have a hard time relating to your cool, reserved style that they perceive as overly focused and literal.
CV.suggestions: Because you can be hard to read, counteract the tendency to be seen as disinterested by nodding and using eye contact. Try to value Connective Listeners as allies who can appreciate your literal thinking and add emotional interest for others.
