RV.atBest: You and Reflective Listeners will tend to appreciate each other’s expertise and respect your mutually reserved style. You, in turn, can bring an analytical eye to help sharpen other Reflective Listeners’ thinking and move it forward toward practical solutions.
RV.challenges: You can each assume you know the right answer without probing one another’s thinking for arriving at a decision. Together, you may get bogged down in “what is” while missing possibilities of what could be.
RV.suggestions: Ask Reflective Listeners about previous experiences they’ve had relevant to the current situation to solicit their input and see if you’re on the same page. Since you’ll be tempted to move forward into problem solving mode before most Reflective Listeners have had a chance to digest all the information, remember to give them extra time to consider all the angles.

AL.atBest: You will tend to work well together, each respecting the other’s rational approach to problem solving. Together, you can set aside your biases and get right to the heart of an issue without distraction.
AL.challenges: You may tend to get bogged down in details without keeping track of the big picture. Because you’re each task-and-solutions oriented, you can work diligently on different aspects of a problem but forget to consult each other and share information to make sure you’re coordinating effectively.
AL.suggestions: While you probably prefer sticking to tasks on your own, regularly coordinate your efforts with each other to maximize your effectiveness. When brainstorming, provide yourselves with clearly defined priorities and outcomes with the big picture always in mind so you don’t get stuck in the weeds.

CL.atBest: Conceptual Listeners can help infuse your thinking with fresh ideas and offer new possible directions when you get stuck “in the weeds.” You can help bridge the Conceptual Listener’s sense of possibility with structures and practical solutions that can bring their ideas into reality.
CL.challenges: Because you value what is factual, reliable, and known from experience, you may tune out Conceptual Listeners who creatively ideate on possibilities that you deem unrealistic at first glance. Conceptual Listeners may find your literal approach to be stifling and unimaginative.
CL.suggestions: While you may be tempted to ignore or play “devil’s advocate” with Conceptual Listeners, instead ask practical questions about how they might get from here to there. Value Conceptual Listeners as a helpful counter-balance that can offer creativity and imagination to your fact-based approach.

name: The Realist
intro: As a REALIST, you rely on Reflective Listening in combination with Analytical Listening and Connective Listening. You listen to connect your personal knowledge and interests to a well- reasoned direction that others can follow. You will tend to tune out when people offer opinions and abstractions instead of facts.
shortDescription: Realists rely on Reflective Listening in combination with Analytical Listening and Connective Listening. They listen to connect their personal knowledge and interests to a well- reasoned direction that others can follow. They will tend to tune out when people offer opinions and abstractions instead of facts.
strengths: You tend to be respected for your realistic points of view. In meetings, you are measured in your contributions, addressing both facts and feelings that come into play. You relate facts and data to actual—not theoretical—problems, and examine how your own prior experiences can inform the issues at hand. You think about the details needed to make well-informed and thorough decisions, and take the needs of others into consideration.
possibleChallenges: With a lower preference for Conceptual Listening, you may stick with known solutions before giving fresh thinking a fair chance. You may also ask for more concrete evidence than the situation requires, which could stifle innovation. You probably prefer to think things through before sharing your thoughts with others, which might keep others from helping set the direction, unless someone speaks rationally and with authority. In this case you could be overly swayed by them or fall under their influence. Once you are convinced of a direction, you are probably closed off to other alternatives.
insights: Bring idea people into direction-setting situations to broaden the scope and options of what to do next.
Ask yourself and others what unintended consequence might occur with proposed solutions.
Share your thinking early in the process so others can follow your train of thought and be included.
Before moving forward, put yourself in the position of those who will be affected by the direction you are setting to avoid pushback at a later time.
Monitor the amount of facts and details you ask for, as this may be perceived as a stalling technique.

CV.atBest: You will tend to get along with Connective Listeners and respect the “people” focus they bring to problem-solving. Connective listeners, in turn, will tend to respect your balanced, measured approach that considers both facts and feelings when finding solutions.
CV.challenges: You may tune out or ignore Connective Listeners who begin to appeal to emotions or show themselves to be biased in their views. Connective Listeners might get frustrated when your approach seems too “procedural” or when you’re overly reserved in sharing your thinking.
CV.suggestions: Share your thinking early so Connective Listeners can feel included in the process. Outline how your proposed options will engage a wide range of constituencies to help Connective Listeners see the value of your thinking.

RV.atBest: You and Reflective Listeners will tend to appreciate each other’s expertise and respect your mutually reserved style. You can bring an analytical eye and “systems view” to help refine other Reflective Listeners’ thinking and move it forward toward practical solutions.
RV.challenges: With a mutually reserved approach, you might spend too much time probing into problems without arriving at practical solutions. Together, you may get bogged down in “what is” while missing possibilities of what could be.
RV.suggestions: While you probably prefer sticking to tasks on your own, regularly coordinate your efforts with each other to avoid blind spots and maximize your effectiveness. When brainstorming, provide yourselves with clearly defined priorities and outcomes with the big picture always in mind so you don’t get stuck in the weeds.

AL.atBest: You will tend to work well together, each respecting the other’s rational approach to problem solving. Together, you can set aside your biases and get right to the heart of an issue without distraction.
AL.challenges: When working together, you may tend to get bogged down in details without keeping track of the big picture. You may be vulnerable to persuasion by Analytical Listeners who speak rationally, convincingly, and with strong authority.
AL.suggestions: When brainstorming, provide yourselves with clearly defined priorities and outcomes with the big picture always in mind so you don’t get stuck in the weeds. If you find yourself “going along” with convincing Analytical Listeners, make an effort to double-check all the facts for yourself.

CL.atBest: Conceptual Listeners can offer fresh, creative ideas to supplement your “realist” thinking and help pull you out of the weeds when needed. You can help bridge the Conceptual Listener’s sense of possibility with structures and practical solutions that can bring their ideas into reality.
CL.challenges: Because you value what is factual, reliable, and known from experience, you may tune out Conceptual Listeners who creatively ideate on possibilities that you deem unrealistic at first glance. Conceptual Listeners may find your literal approach to be stifling and unimaginative.
CL.suggestions: While you may be tempted to ignore or play “devil’s advocate” with Conceptual Listeners, instead ask practical questions about how they might get from here to there. Value Conceptual Listeners as a helpful counter-balance that can offer creativity and imagination to your habitually “realist” approach.

name: The Synthesizer
intro: As a SYNTHESIZER, you rely on Reflective Listening in combination with Analytical Listening and Conceptual Listening. You listen for unusual ways to build upon knowledge. You will tend to tune out when you cannot develop a variety of ideas that will lead to innovations.
shortDescription: Synthesizers rely on Reflective Listening in combination with Analytical Listening and Conceptual Listening. They listen for unusual ways to build upon knowledge. They will tend to tune out when they cannot develop a variety of ideas that will lead to innovations.
strengths: With a high preference for Reflective Listening, you are interested in expanding personal experiences. Additionally, with your combination of Analytical and Conceptual Listening, you use information from a wide array of sources to create new learning and options. You balance what is, facts, with what might be, ideas, and are not swayed by emotions. You are open to new ideas, but will also scrutinize them for supporting facts and evidence before fully accepting them.
possibleChallenges: Due to your low preference for Connective Listening, you tend to pay attention to your self- interests more than the interests of others—both internal and external customers. You probably take an intellectual approach to conversations, and may play “devil’s advocate” for your own enjoyment, rather than to solicit other people’s ideas. When dealing with an issue, you may overlook the interpersonal context or any relationship dynamics that may be at play. You will tend to focus on the details of what is being said rather than who is speaking. You may also ignore information from untapped sources that could add value.
insights: Gain full understanding of expectations and needs of others before starting an interaction.
Separate fact-checking from idea-generation and tell the speaker(s) which you are listening for in the interaction.
Articulate your internal conversation so others can follow your train of thought and understand how you arrived at your conclusion.
Write down your ideas as well as supportive details, and share them with others who have a vested interest in your thinking.
Avoid peppering people with objections and questions. Space them out and ask open-ended questions to allow others to contribute in the interaction.

CV.atBest: Connective Listeners can help sell your ideas while making sure they take into consideration all the relevant constituencies that may be affected by them. You can think methodically through and analyze a Connective Listener’s inspirations to help show what’s realistic and what should be jettisoned.
CV.challenges: Connective Listeners may perceive you as overly intellectual or self-involved without showing enough interest in others. You may be unimpressed by Connective Listeners who bring a highly relational or emotional approach to interactions.
CV.suggestions: Outline clearly how your thinking will benefit or affect all the constituencies involved to help Connective Listeners see the value of your ideas and become advocates. Actively seek input from Connective Listeners to provide an important supplement to your otherwise intellectual thinking.

RV.atBest: You and Reflective Listeners will tend to appreciate each other’s expertise and respect your mutual tendency to be un-swayed by appeals to emotions. You can bring an analytical eye as well as fresh ideas that help inspire Reflective Listeners to move their thinking forward.
RV.challenges: Reflective Listeners may get frustrated with your tendency to move quickly into ideation or problem- solving before they’ve had a chance to consider the issue fully. You may wonder why the Reflective Listener isn’t contributing their thoughts and ideas as readily as you.
RV.suggestions: Don’t expect Reflective Listeners to jump in as quickly as you; give them extra time to consider all the angles before offering their well-considered thoughts. As a way to engage them, ask Reflective Listeners about previous experiences they’ve had relevant to the current situation.

AL.atBest: You will tend to work well together, combining your respective rational and intellectual approaches to arrive at practical solutions to problems. Together, you can set aside your biases and focus on the facts as they pertain to the issue at hand.
AL.challenges: You may tune out when Analytical Listeners seem too detail oriented and lose sight of the bigger picture. They may get frustrated when you begin ideating around the issue instead of sticking to the hard data.
AL.suggestions: When speaking with Analytical Listeners, back up your ideas with relevant facts and data to help them see the value of your thinking. If Analytical Listeners start probing your ideas, welcome it as an opportunity to ferret out any weak points.

CL.atBest: Conceptual Listeners can help augment your thinking with fresh ideas and new, creative possibilities. You can help bridge the Conceptual Listener’s sense of possibility with structures and practical solutions that can bring their ideas into reality.
CL.challenges: While you value possibilities, you also value what is factual, reliable, and known from experience, so you may tune out Conceptual Listeners who creatively ideate on possibilities that you deem unrealistic at first glance. When you do ideate well together, you can still tend to leave out the “people” component and forget to consider how your ideas may impact other people, departments, or constituencies.
CL.suggestions: While you may be tempted to play “devil’s advocate” with Conceptual Listeners, instead ask practical questions about how they might get from here to there. When brainstorming with Conceptual Listeners, make a concerted effort to determine how your ideas would impact other potentially affected groups.

name: The Ingester
intro: As an INGESTER, you rely on Reflective Listening in combination with Conceptual Listening. You listen for ideas that will expand your knowledge and experience. You will tend to tune out when nothing new is shared that stirs your curiosity.
shortDescription: Ingesters rely on Reflective Listening in combination with Conceptual Listening. They listen for ideas that will expand their knowledge and experience. They will tend to tune out when nothing new is shared that stirs their curiosity.
strengths: You are a highly reflective listener with a strong preference to conceptualize, as well, so you use existing knowledge and experience to come up with new ideas. You are patient and allow interactions to unfold naturally. You are also open minded, and tend not to judge people or concepts and therefore can be a particularly helpful thinking partner during brainstorming sessions. Before making complex decisions, you will always take time to weigh in your personal knowledge and experience.
possibleChallenges: You probably spend a lot of time in your head. You can generate ideas, reflect on them, and generate more without producing results. Because you tend to cherish your own ideas, you may let your personal knowledge prevail without looking beyond it, even when the situation calls for fresh thinking. Although you enjoy brainstorming, you probably don’t like to do it in large groups; while you have a lot of ideas, you may not be quick to share them.
insights: If you tend to hold back, speak up. Do not wait to be invited to share ideas.
If there are too many people talking at once for you to be comfortable chiming in, write ideas to offer later.
Be aware of the signals your body language is giving off to others.
Encourage others to generate ideas along with you.
Ask abstract questions such as, “How might we understand what’s underlying this problem?” or “What do you think is the real problem we’re dealing with?"

CV.atBest: You may not always be quick to share ideas, but you will likely find it natural to share your ideas with Connective Listeners, and they, in turn, will find it natural to listen. Connective Listeners can take your ideas and show how they might affect other relevant people, customers, or processes in ways you hadn’t considered.
CV.challenges: You and Connective Listeners can approach an interaction with very different purposes, creating a sense of tension that the Connective Listener will more naturally take the responsibility for smoothing over. You enjoy ideating, but a strong Connective Listener may not share the same enthusiasm unless the ideas have immediate relevance to—or benefit for—others.
CV.suggestions: When you’re not ready to share ideas, don’t hesitate to let Connective Listeners know you need more time to consider the issue at hand. They’ll respect this. Frame your ideas in a way that highlights their effect on other people to help Connective Listeners understand the value of your thinking.

RV.atBest: Together, you share a mutual appreciation for knowledge and understanding and can explore overlapping areas of expertise at length. Since you both have a reserved style, you’ll respect each other’s time to process your thinking before speaking.
RV.challenges: You may be tempted to spend too much time probing interesting subjects or areas of expertise rather than moving forward with the task-at-hand. You could assume that because of similarity in thinking you’re on the right track.
RV.suggestions: When brainstorming, provide yourselves with clearly defined priorities and outcomes which can serve as a framework within which to better focus thinking & ideation. Before you get too far, ask yourself and each other how your ideas might impact others, and what additional facts or data you would need to move forward.

AL.atBest: You can offer an Analytical Listener fresh ideas and different perspectives collected from your own experience. Analytical Listeners can help serve as a grounding force for your ideas, often by asking probing questions to see where they hold up.
AL.challenges: Analytical Listeners may feel impatient with your ideas if you don’t ground them in facts, data, or practicality. You may feel annoyed by their literal approach and what you perceive as a lack of imagination.
AL.suggestions: Try not to take the Analytical Listener’s probing as a statement of personal mistrust. Rather, value them as a counterweight that brings balance to your thinking. When speaking to Analytical Listeners, support your ideas with facts, data, and practical details to help them understand the importance of your thinking.

CL.atBest: You will speak naturally into each other’s listening and are therefore likely to feel you are on the same wavelength. Together, you can foster mutual creativity and ideation with almost perpetual stamina.
CL.challenges: Your greatest mutual strength could become a handicap when time is short and you’re unable to translate your thinking into a quick decision. You might get “stuck in the clouds” together, constantly considering new angles on an issue, without enough grounding in real-time facts, data, or resource considerations.
CL.suggestions: When brainstorming, provide yourselves with a clear list of priorities and a timeframe that will force you to “reign in” your thinking. With your preference for Reflective Listening, you can further help ground conversations by speaking from your own experience of what works and what doesn’t.

name: The Pacesetter
intro: As a PACESETTER, you rely on Reflective Listening in combination with Conceptual Listening and Connective Listening. You listen for ideas that create momentum for you as well as for others. You will tend to tune out when the pace gets too slow from an over-abundance of details.
shortDescription: Pacesetters rely on Reflective Listening in combination with Conceptual Listening and Connective Listening. They listen for ideas that create momentum for they as well as for others. They will tend to tune out when the pace gets too slow from an over-abundance of details.
strengths: With your high preference for Reflective, Conceptual, and Connective Listening, you gather knowledge, information, and experiences from multiple sources to generate ideas. You have an open mind and absorb input from many sources, but will trust your own past experiences first and foremost to inform future direction. In meetings, you will tend to pay attention to the big picture and potential options while balancing your own personal interests with the needs of others. Once information is gathered, you will move into action, setting the pace for the rest of the team.
possibleChallenges: You may enjoy considering more ideas than can realistically be executed. Your love of brainstorming (alone or with others) has the potential to drag on without application, and you may become absorbed in ideas without providing practical solutions. However, your preference for Connective Listening may help pull you out of your own head, and sometimes even have the reverse-effect of moving you into action too quickly. Your low preference for Analytical Listening may lead you to overlook details and facts, resulting in premature action. You may also be swayed by others’ opinions without stopping to ask for supportive data.
insights: Share your ideas and ask others for theirs.
Balance the need to listen for your own self-interests as well as for the interests of others.
Take note of details that can help you make more informed decisions.
Establish the purpose for and amount of time to spend on idea-generation.
Check yourself to see whether you are weighing information based on the data or by who is presenting it.

CV.atBest: You will tend to get along well with Connective Listeners and respect the “people” focus they bring to problem-solving. Connective listeners will tend to respect the interesting ideas you offer which consider multiple points of view while taking various constituencies under consideration.
CV.challenges: You may tend to tune out or ignore Connective Listeners whose biased views or appeals to emotions get in the way of forward progress. Connective Listeners may get frustrated if you begin to base your approach too much in what you already know rather than collecting further information from others involved.
CV.suggestions: Value Connective Listeners as a helpful resource that will signal whether you’re beginning to move forward too quickly without considering all the interested parties. When brainstorming with Connective Listeners, try to ground your ideas in facts and available data to mitigate the possibility of “ungrounded” ideating together.

RV.atBest: You will tend to appreciate each other’s expertise and respect your mutually thoughtful style. You can bring an analytical eye and “systems view” to help refine other Reflective Listeners’ thinking and move it forward toward practical solutions.
RV.challenges: You may enjoy ideating and tossing out ideas more rapidly than most Reflective Listeners, who may resort to tuning you out if they don’t have time to process all the angles. You, in turn, could simply ignore Reflective Listeners who aren’t speaking up and adding to the ideas and options under consideration.
RV.suggestions: Always give Reflective Listeners plenty of time and space to digest and process their thinking; the wait is usually worth it. If you need them to engage, ask Reflective Listeners if they’ve had previous experiences that might help inform the issue at hand.

AL.atBest: Analytical Listeners can help balance your preference for knowledge gathering and idea generating with an eye for relevant facts, data, and practical resources. You can take an Analytical Listener’s practical findings, incorporate them with current needs and keep the process moving forward.
AL.challenges: Because you trust what you’ve learned from experience and like moving forward with good ideas, you may get frustrated with Analytical Listeners who seem overly literal in their approach or lost in the details. They may question the validity of some of your ideas and possibly probe them for signs of weakness.
AL.suggestions: Try to value the Analytical Listener’s “devil’s advocate” approach as an important way to sharpen your thinking and make sure all relevant facts have been taken into consideration. When speaking to Analytical Listeners, support your ideas with relevant facts, data, or other statistics to help them understand the value of your thinking.

CL.atBest: You’ll appreciate the ideation of Conceptual Listeners while checking their ideas against your own experience and considering their relevance for others. Conceptual Listeners will help bolster your own thinking with a fresh sense of ideas and possibilities.
CL.challenges: Together, you may share a good “brainstorming” rapport but it may also lack grounding in facts, details, and a discerning eye for what’s practical given the situation and resources. You may begin to tune out Conceptual Listeners who want to keep ideating on possibilities when you’re ready to move toward implementation.
CL.suggestions: When brainstorming, provide yourselves with clearly defined priorities and outcomes which can serve as a framework within which to better focus thinking and ideation. With your preference for Reflective Listening, you can further help ground conversations by speaking from your own experience of what works and what doesn’t.

name: The Investigator
intro: As an INVESTIGATOR, you rely on Reflective Listening in combination with Conceptual Listening and Analytical Listening. You listen for well thought-out ideas to shed light on your situation. You will tend to tune out when other people intervene without reason.
shortDescription: Investigators rely on Reflective Listening in combination with Conceptual Listening and Analytical Listening. They listen for well thought-out ideas to shed light on their situation. They will tend to tune out when other people intervene without reason.
strengths: You are probably comfortable thinking and deliberating in your head. You easily take in both logical and abstract information, then determine how the information provided is relevant to you. You will have plenty of ideas and details to offer to the conversation, but with your high preference for Reflective Listening, you will tend to pause before speaking. You are a balanced thinker, considering both opinions and facts when making decisions. You are comfortable focusing on the big picture as well as minute details. When brainstorming, you will examine the relevance of options as they pertain to actual issues at hand.
possibleChallenges: With your low preference for Connective Listening, you will tend to hold thoughts internally and be slow to share them with the group. You may fail to recognize a decision’s impact on other people, departments, offices, and organizations. You probably rely on your own experience and knowledge when making decisions, and don’t involve others as much as you could. Your introspective nature may also slow things down when quick decisions are required; you might simply turn ideas over and over without determining the best options given the available details.
insights: Take note of relevant information that can be useful to others and share it.
Ask questions about how the information might impact decisions and actions of others.
Share what you are thinking, even if it seems tedious to have to articulate what you’ve already thought through in your head.
Offer your ideas and supporting facts as information only and not necessarily the “right” decision.
Include the experience and knowledge of others in your decision-making.

CV.atBest: You may not always be quick to share ideas, but Connective Listeners will tend to listen with interest and learn from your critical observations. Connective Listeners can draw out your ideas and show how they might affect other relevant people, customers, or processes in ways you hadn’t considered.
CV.challenges: Connective Listeners may feel uncomfortable when you hold your thoughts internally and don’t share them with the group. You may tune out Connective Listeners when they seem too emotionally invested in a topic or too eager to have everyone contribute.
CV.suggestions: Try to regularly share your thoughts and considerations, even if you haven’t arrived at a conclusion. Articulate how your solutions would affect other people and constituencies in order to help Connective Listeners see the value of your thinking.

RV.atBest: Together, you share a mutual appreciation for knowledge and understanding and can explore overlapping areas of expertise at length. With your similarly reserved style, you’ll respect each other’s time to process your thinking before speaking.
RV.challenges: You may be tempted to spend too much time probing interesting subjects or questions rather than moving forward with the task-at-hand. You could assume that because of similarity in thinking you’re on the right track.
RV.suggestions: Share what you are thinking, even if it seems tedious to go through what you’ve already worked out in your head. Before you get too far, ask yourself and each other how your ideas might impact other stakeholders— team members, clients, business partners, etc.

AL.atBest: Analytical Listeners will tend to appreciate your balanced approach with your ability to set emotions aside to arrive at reasoned conclusions. You will generally appreciate the Analytical Listener’s highly rational approach and value their focus on facts and data as an important component of decision-making.
AL.challenges: While working together, you can easily forget to consider how your ideas may affect other people or constituencies. Highly Analytical Listeners may tune you out when you speak what you know but don’t take the time to back your knowledge with proof.
AL.suggestions: While brainstorming, make an effort to consider how your solutions would affect all the people and constituencies involved. Since you more often speak from personal experience, back your thinking with facts and data to help Analytical Listeners better understand the value of your ideas.

CL.atBest: You and Conceptual Listeners will typically enjoy good brainstorming rapport and feel you are on the same wavelength. Additionally, you can help Conceptual Listeners identify where they need more facts and data while offering your own insights from personal experience.
CL.challenges: You may tune out Conceptual Listeners when they ideate on too many possibilities that aren’t supported by facts or by what you’ve come to understand through your own experience. Together, you may consider numerous angles to approach a problem while forgetting to incorporate the “human” component.
CL.suggestions: When brainstorming, provide yourselves with clearly defined priorities and outcomes which can serve as a framework to better focus thinking and ideation. When you’re tempted to tune out Conceptual Listeners who seem unrealistic, instead ask them how they would get from here to there. ANALYTICAL LISTENING PROFILES

name: The Scrutinizer
strengths: As a highly Analytical Listener, you discern the validity of information being presented, and are quick to point out information that may be imprecise. You also critique the decision-making process for adherence to standards. You listen for facts behind feelings and pay close attention to reason. You control your own personal biases and attitudes, not allowing them to get in the way of rational thinking.
possibleChallenges: Your need to get to a “right” answer may also come across as inflexibility; you may shut down the sharing of information in service of getting to a quick solution. You might prematurely discard information that could otherwise be valuable if it doesn’t come from a source you approve of. If an idea seems frivolous or off track, you may just ignore it rather than inquire further. In meetings, you will tend to sacrifice feelings for facts and therefore can miss otherwise important nuances and undertones of the communication exchange.
insights: Interact to demonstrate your interest in people as well as in details. Use body language, voice and eye contact to show engagement.
Share what you are thinking with others.
Balance your comments equally between what looks right as well as what is of concern.
Ask “what if” questions that might not be fact-seeking. For example, “What unintended consequences could arise that would impact this decision?”
Examine how you can apply others’ input to a task you are working on.

CV.atBest: You help Connective Listeners ground their “people-oriented” focus in facts, data, and practical applications. Connective Listeners can help you consider how your analyses and researched opinions may affect others while helping you think through the best method of delivery.
CV.challenges: Given your divergent priorities, Connective Listeners may tune out when you stick to facts and details if you do not consider enough of a “people” component. You may consider their prioritization of other people and their emotions to be impractical or unrealistic.
CV.suggestions: Resist the temptation to disregard Connective Listeners who seem too soft or idealistic; they can build bridges and help others to appreciate your approach. When sharing your opinions with Connective Listeners, they’ll be more likely to see things your way if you show you’ve considered the impact on other people and constituencies.

RV.atBest: You will tend to appreciate the personal knowledge and thoughtful perspectives Reflective Listeners bring to interactions. They will tend to appreciate your logical approach and will trust you to vet their ideas for practicality.
RV.challenges: You may grow impatient with Reflective Listeners when you are sure about something but they are still contemplating the information in their heads. They may get turned off when your responses seem too quick and literal-minded to show that you've appreciated depth of the situation.
RV.suggestions: Always give Reflective Listeners space and time to formulate their thoughts. If you need them to engage, ask if they've had previous experiences that may be applicable to the current situation.

AL.atBest: You will tend to share a mutual respect for facts, data, and details, and your like-mindedness will tend to make you good work-partners. Together, you can set aside your biases and get right to the heart of an issue without distraction.
AL.challenges: You may tend to get bogged down in details without keeping track of the big picture. Because you’re each task-and-solutions oriented, you can work diligently on different aspects of a problem but forget to consult each other and share information to make sure you’re coordinating effectively.
AL.suggestions: While you probably prefer sticking to tasks on your own, regularly coordinate your efforts with each other to maximize your co-creative effectiveness. When brainstorming, provide yourselves with clearly defined priorities and outcomes with the big picture always in mind so you don’t get stuck in the weeds.

CL.atBest: You can help Conceptual Listeners ground their big ideas and inspirations in facts and details while vetting what is realistic and what isn’t. Conceptual Listeners, in turn, can offer a sense of imagination and fresh direction when you get bogged down in details.
CL.challenges: Because you highly value what is true, factual, and measurable, you may become frustrated with Conceptual Listeners who like to ideate on possibilities. Conceptual Listeners, in turn, may find your literal approach to be stifling and unimaginative.
CL.suggestions: While you may be tempted to play “devil’s advocate” with Conceptual Listeners, instead ask practical questions about how they might get from here to there. Value Conceptual Listeners as a helpful counter-balance that can offer creativity and imagination to your fact-based approach.

name: The Interpreter
intro: As an INTERPRETER, you rely on Analytical Listening in combination with Connective Listening. You listen for ways to overcome resistance when setting direction. You will tend to tune out when irrelevant ideas threaten to take progress off track.
shortDescription: Interpreters rely on Analytical Listening in combination with Connective Listening. They listen for ways to overcome resistance when setting direction. They will tend to tune out when irrelevant ideas threaten to take progress off track.
strengths: With a high preference for Analytical and Connective Listening, you are interested in problem solving and are able to collaborate with others to get to well-reasoned solutions. You focus in on the relevant information, enroll others when executing direction, and filter out details that do not include consideration of people. You also have a strong ability to translate & interpret technical details for others to understand.
possibleChallenges: If there is not a clear path towards a solution to the problem at hand, you may tend to disengage. When involved in problem solving, you may ask for endless amounts of details that might turn people off, and you may provide more facts and data during “translation” mode than truly needed or desired. Once you have decided on a direction, you may be prone to taking pushback personally and withdrawing from those who question your decisions. You can tend to form allegiances with trusted, like-minded people, and may overlook others who can add value. You may also sometimes feel torn between what you are thinking and what you are feeling.
insights: Focus on what is being said in addition to who is saying it and value both equally.

Take notes that capture facts and details while you are considering how the information can be applied to others. Do this by dividing a page in half, write FACTS at the top of one side and PEOPLE on the other. Then draw a line across the bottom and write SOLUTIONS. Use this guide to gather information and form direction.
Ask detailed questions to bring others who do not listen like you into the sphere of the issue.
Limit the amount of facts and data you throw at a decision just to prove your point. Don’t wear down others in service of a preferred solution.
Ask clarifying questions when people disagree with you and thank them for their willingness to speak up.

CV.atBest: You’ll add value to your interactions with Connective Listeners when you balance their people-focus with your desire to probe into facts and data. Your likely openness toward each other will encourage collaborative problem-solving and fresh analysis of the issue at hand.
CV.challenges: You may feel torn between facts and feelings when dealing with Connective Listeners, especially because you have an allegiance to both, but you may eventually tune out Connective Listeners who overly appeal to emotions. Alternatively, you may simply tune out Connective Listeners who don’t ground any of their reasoning in facts.
CV.suggestions: Watch for any tendency to appease or ignore Connective Listeners by appearing to agree with them while not taking them seriously. Before writing them off, use thoughtful questions to draw in Connective Listeners and demonstrate your interest in their ideas through positive body language.

RV.atBest: Reflective Listeners will enrich the exchange with useful insights and ideas based on their knowledge and experience. You can offer a critical eye to help sharpen Reflective Listener’s thoughts in a non-invasive way.
RV.challenges: Because you tend to move forward toward solutions, you may grow impatient with Reflective Listeners who take too long to process their internal thinking and “get on board.” Reflective Listeners may think you tend to move to quickly ahead without considering all the angles.
RV.suggestions: Always allow Reflective Listeners plenty of time to process their thinking before pressuring them to move forward. Count on Reflective Listeners as important resources who may have quietly and carefully considered angles you hadn’t, especially when they have previous experience in the subject under consideration.

AL.atBest: You will tend to share a mutual respect for facts, data, and details, and your like-mindedness will tend to make you good work-partners. You can also help Analytical Listeners consider how their analyses and researched opinions will “land” with others.
AL.challenges: Together, you may tend to get bogged down in details without keeping track of the big picture. As stated above, you may feel torn between facts and feelings and become frustrated with Analytical Listeners who aren’t considering enough of a “people” component.
AL.suggestions: To curb your tendency to get “stuck in the weeds” together, set a clear agenda with timing and priorities when collaborating. If you feel yourself being pulled by the Analytical Listener toward an approach that ignores a “people” component, champion its importance with relevant data, statistics, or anecdotes to help them understand your reasoning.

CL.atBest: You can appreciate the fresh ideas and possibilities offered by Conceptual Listeners while helping them move toward practical implementation. Conceptual Listeners can help take your ideas to imaginative levels you hadn’t considered.
CL.challenges: Even though you might appear to be listening, you may prematurely tune out Conceptual Listeners whose ideas seem impractical at first blush. Conceptual Listeners may get frustrated with your literal approach that, in their view, might not be incorporating enough imaginative thinking.
CL.suggestions: Watch out for any tendency to nod in agreement with Conceptual Listeners without taking them seriously and instead ask questions that can help them ground their thinking. Seek out Conceptual Listeners as partners who can add creative new dimensions to solutions you’re already considering.

name: The Researcher
intro: As a RESEARCHER, you rely on Analytical Listening in combination with Connective Listening and Reflective Listening. You listen for specific information that will enhance the breadth of knowledge and experience for yourself and others. You will tend to tune out when the information is deemed frivolous.
shortDescription: Researchers rely on Analytical Listening in combination with Connective Listening and Reflective Listening. They listen for specific information that will enhance the breadth of knowledge and experience for themself and others. They will tend to tune out when the information is deemed frivolous.
strengths: You care that your knowledge, experience, and probing mind will prove to be of value to situations that are in need of critical thinking. Whatever you uncover must be valid before you will suggest a particular solution to anyone, and because of that, you will discard questionable information rather than allowing it to adversely affect people and situations within your purview.
possibleChallenges: Your high preference for Analytical Listening could lead you to move too quickly for others in your rush to get to answers. You might cut off people delivering information if their presentation is not focused or clear. However, your Connective Listening makes you more sensitive to others, so you may get torn between wanting nothing but the facts while also caring about the impact on people, which could cause confusion. You may also tend to develop allies and exclude others who might not think the same way.
insights: Watch others’ body language to determine if they appear to be following at your pace.
Take notes to reduce the likelihood you will interrupt others before they have completed their thoughts.
When there is an information gap, stop the process to learn what is happening and then rectify it.
Be clear about what you want to learn as well as why, and how you will go about gaining the knowledge.
Go to people you don’t ordinarily access and ask for their thoughts.

CV.atBest: Connective Listeners will tend to appreciate the practical insights and experienced, solutions-oriented approach you bring to the interaction. You will tend to respect the Connective Listener’s concern to make sure all people and processes are suﬃciently considered.
CV.challenges: You may tune out Connective Listeners if they begin to appeal to emotions and advocate approaches that aren’t suﬃciently grounded in supportive details. Connective Listeners may begin to tune you out if they perceive you dwelling on endless facts and details that may be interesting to you, but not clearly relevant to everyone else.
CV.suggestions: Instead of tuning out Connective Listeners who aren’t moving quickly enough, ask them to explain the reasoning behind their position so you can better understand it. Watch for the tendency to surround yourself with like-minded people interested in the same topics, but who might not challenge your thinking, either.

RV.atBest: You’ll appreciate Reflective Listeners who enrich the exchange with useful insights and ideas based on their knowledge and experience. You can also help Reflective Listeners fit all their wide-ranging thoughts into a well-organized system.
RV.challenges: Because you tend to have a bias toward action, you may grow impatient if Reflective Listeners take too long to process their internal thinking before “getting on board.” Reflective Listeners may think you tend to move too quickly ahead without considering all the angles.
RV.suggestions: Always allow Reflective Listeners plenty of time to process their thinking before pressuring them to move forward. Count on Reflective Listeners as important resources who may have quietly and carefully considered angles you hadn’t, especially when they have previous experience in the subject under consideration.

AL.atBest: You will tend to make good work-partners, sharing a mutual reliance on facts, data, and details to advance yourself toward solutions. Your Connective side can help Analytical Listeners consider how their analyses and researched opinions will “land” with others.
AL.challenges: You can both lose sight of the big picture and pay attention only to the details that inform the topic that holds your interest. You also may feel torn between facts and feelings and become frustrated with Analytical Listeners who aren’t considering a “people” component.
AL.suggestions: To curb your tendency to get “stuck in the weeds” together, provide time for blue-sky thinking that will pull you out and into possibilities. If you feel yourself being pulled by the Analytical Listener toward an approach that ignores a “people” component, champion its importance with relevant data, statistics, or anecdotes to help them understand your reasoning.

CL.atBest: You can appreciate the fresh ideas and possibilities offered by Conceptual Listeners while helping them move toward practical implementation. Conceptual Listeners can help lift your thinking to imaginative levels you hadn’t considered.
CL.challenges: You may tune out Conceptual Listeners whose ideas seem impractical at first blush, but due to your “Connective” side, you’ll probably try not to be rude about it. Conceptual Listeners, in turn, may get frustrated with your literal “problem-solving” thinking that moves forward without incorporating enough imagination or creativity.
CL.suggestions: Watch out for any tendency to nod in agreement with Conceptual Listeners without taking them seriously and instead ask questions that can help them ground their thinking. Seek out Conceptual Listeners as partners who can add creative new dimensions to solutions you’re already considering.

name: The Surveyor
intro: As a SURVEYOR, you rely on Analytical Listening in combination with Connective Listening and Conceptual Listening. You listen for the problem and who is involved and affected. You will tend to tune out when requested to provide personal information to be used in decision-making.
shortDescription: Surveyors rely on Analytical Listening in combination with Connective Listening and Conceptual Listening. They listen for the problem and who is involved and affected. They will tend to tune out when requested to provide personal information to be used in decision-making.
strengths: You tend to have a broad view that provides objectivity when assessing a situation. You balance facts and data with new and different ideas, and you also pay attention to the concerns and needs of people who may be touched by the situation under consideration. You easily collaborate with people whose knowledge and experience might add value to deliberations, and you tend not to impose your own point-of-view on others.
possibleChallenges: With a low preference for Reflective Listening, you may neglect to look inward, ignoring your own relevant experience and knowledge. Although you collaborate with others, you may tend to prefer only a trusted few who also ground their ideas in facts and details. You might not share your thinking and feelings beyond that select few, which could keep others at a distance. You also might request more details and data than a given situation truly calls for, and may get caught between wanting proof and striving for breakthrough thinking.
insights: Examine your past experiences and knowledge to determine how they might have bearing on what is at hand.
Include others in your reflections and ask for their feedback.
Broaden the base of people you include in the problem-solving process.
When trying to understand the complexities of a problem, ask questions, but be careful not to interrogate others in the process.
Prioritize the information you need from others and resist adding to the list during the process.

CV.atBest: Connective Listeners will tend to appreciate the interesting ideas and experienced, solutions-oriented approach you bring to the interaction. You will tend to respect the Connective Listener’s concern to make sure all people and processes are suﬃciently considered.
CV.challenges: While you listen with an open mind, you may tune out Connective Listeners if they begin to overly appeal to emotions or seem biased in their views. Connective Listeners may be frustrated when you seem overly focused on grounding the discussion in facts and details.
CV.suggestions: Instead of tuning out Connective Listeners who aren’t providing enough facts, ask them to explain the reasoning behind their position so you can better understand it. Openly include Connective Listeners in your thinking and regularly ask for their feedback to ensure you’re keeping all constituencies in consideration.

RV.atBest: You’ll appreciate Reflective Listeners who enrich the exchange with useful insights and ideas based on their knowledge and experience. You can help Reflective Listeners vet their ideas for practicality and further possibilities.
RV.challenges: You may grow impatient if Reflective Listeners take too long to process their internal thinking before offering their feedback. Reflective Listeners may think you tend to move too quickly toward conclusions (even if you don’t share them right away).
RV.suggestions: Always allow Reflective Listeners plenty of time to process their thinking before pressuring them to move forward. Count on Reflective Listeners as important resources who may have quietly and carefully considered angles you hadn’t, especially when they have previous experience in the subject under consideration.

AL.atBest: You will tend to share a mutual respect for facts, data, and details, and you will tend to make good work- partners. You can also help Analytical Listeners consider how to best present their analyses for others while considering new ideas their data may point to.
AL.challenges: While you value facts and logical thinking, you may get frustrated with and tune out Analytical Listeners who don’t also consider a “people” component. Analytical Listeners may be intrigued by your sensitivity to people while you also seem to be so factual and process oriented.
AL.suggestions: If you feel yourself being pulled by the Analytical Listener toward an approach that ignores a “people” component, champion its importance with relevant data, statistics, or anecdotes to help them understand your reasoning. When collaborating with Analytical Listeners, make sure to reflect on past experiences that may provide insights toward the present situation.

CL.atBest: You can appreciate the fresh ideas and possibilities offered by Conceptual Listeners while helping them move toward practical implementation. Conceptual Listeners can help take your ideas to imaginative levels you might not have considered.
CL.challenges: Even though you’ll probably appear to be listening, you may prematurely tune out Conceptual Listeners whose ideas seem impractical at first blush. Conceptual Listeners may get frustrated with your practical approach that, in their view, might not be incorporating enough imaginative thinking.
CL.suggestions: Watch out for any tendency to nod in agreement with Conceptual Listeners without taking them seriously and instead ask questions that can help them ground their thinking. When you do strike a good brainstorming chord, facts and ideas will come naturally, but make an effort to incorporate your own personal knowledge from past experience as well.

name: The Definer
intro: As a DEFINER, you rely on Analytical Listening in combination with Reflective Listening. You listen to understand the information at a deep level. You will tend to tune out when substance is lacking.
shortDescription: Definers rely on Analytical Listening in combination with Reflective Listening. They listen to understand the information at a deep level. They will tend to tune out when substance is lacking.
strengths: With a high preference for both Analytical and Reflective Listening, you connect problems and issues to what is personally known or experienced. You take time to reflect on information rather than jumping to conclusions. You think critically about the relevance of facts and data, and compare them against your own knowledge or expertise. You will easily discard overly personal or biased information from others; you focus on the content of what is being said rather than who is presenting the information.
possibleChallenges: With a relatively low preference for Connective Listening, you probably prefer to solve problems alone, often without consulting others. You might ignore what others can contribute. When you do consult others, you may tend to overlook the feelings behind any information presented, even when those feelings are relevant. You might often require certainty and ask for more facts and data than a given problem requires. You can also get bogged down in “what is” and ignore “what can be.”
insights: Use body language, voice and eye contact to show others you are connecting. You can be hard to read and give an impression of disinterest when you are actually thinking.
Take notes and share them. Do anything that will cause you to engage with others during an interaction.
Ask both fact and feeling questions to a variety of people.
Refrain from grilling people who might have knowledge to share.
Allow the process to unfold rather than rushing it along because you “know the answer.”

CV.atBest: You can help analyze a Connective Listener’s ideas or inspirations and let them know exactly what will work and what won’t. Connective Listeners will ensure your value is understood by others, and explore ways your conclusions can benefit wider constituencies.
CV.challenges: Because you stick to facts, focus on details, and take time to think critically, you may tune out Connective Listeners who seem too relationally focused or biased in their thinking. Connective Listeners may have a hard time relating to your cool, reserved style that they might perceive as overly focused and literal.
CV.suggestions: Because you can be hard to read, counteract this by sharing your thoughts even before they are fully confirmed. Try to value Connective Listeners as allies who can value your literal thinking and help others to appreciate it, too.

RV.atBest: You will tend to appreciate each other’s expertise and respect your mutually reserved style. You can bring an analytical eye to help sharpen other Reflective Listeners’ thinking and move it forward toward practical solutions.
RV.challenges: With a mutually reserved approach, when working on a problem together, you can assume you’re on the right track without knowing exactly what the other is truly thinking. Together, you may get bogged down in “what is” while missing possibilities of what could be.
RV.suggestions: Ask Reflective Listeners about previous experiences they’ve had relevant to the current situation to solicit their input and see if you’re on the same page. Since you’ll be tempted to move forward into problem solving mode before most Reflective Listeners have had a chance to digest all the information, remember to give them extra time to consider all the angles.

AL.atBest: You will tend to work well together, each respecting the other’s rational approach to problem solving. Together, you can set aside your biases and get right to the heart of an issue without distraction.
AL.challenges: You may tend to get bogged down in details without keeping track of the big picture. Because you’re each task-and-solutions oriented, you can work diligently on different aspects of a problem but forget to consult each other and share information to make sure you’re coordinating effectively.
AL.suggestions: While you probably prefer sticking to tasks on your own, regularly coordinate your efforts with each other to avoid blind spots and maximize your effectiveness. When brainstorming, provide yourselves with clearly defined priorities and outcomes with the big picture always in mind so you don’t get stuck “in the weeds.”

CL.atBest: Conceptual Listeners can help infuse your thinking with fresh ideas and offer new possible directions when you hit a quagmire of details and practical considerations. You can help bridge the Conceptual Listener’s sense of possibility with structures and practical solutions that can bring their ideas into reality.
CL.challenges: Because you value what is factual, reliable, and known from experience, you may tune out Conceptual Listeners who creatively ideate on possibilities that you deem unrealistic at first glance. Conceptual Listeners may find your literal approach to be stifling and unimaginative.
CL.suggestions: While you might be tempted to ignore or play “devil’s advocate” with Conceptual Listeners, instead ask practical questions about how they might get from here to there. Value Conceptual Listeners as a helpful counter-balance that can offer creativity and imagination to your fact-based approach.

name: The Producer
intro: As a PRODUCER, you rely on Analytical Listening in combination with Reflective Listening and Connective Listening. You listen for ways to enroll people to follow a particular direction. You will tend to tune out when the direction or buy-in waivers.
shortDescription: Producers rely on Analytical Listening in combination with Reflective Listening and Connective Listening. They listen for ways to enroll people to follow a particular direction. They will tend to tune out when the direction or buy-in waivers.
strengths: You have an eye for problem solving, and connect your own knowledge and experience with the experience of others in your decision-making. You rely on facts and details while simultaneously considering others’ concerns and needs. You clearly see how to resolve situations, and apply logical processes that give others confidence to follow. When you share your own thinking, you support it with reliable data.
possibleChallenges: With a low preference for Conceptual Listening, you may ignore possibly innovative ideas and options when making decisions. You can also lose patience with solutions that do not take the impact of people into consideration. In your pursuit of problem-solving, you might over-invest in resources, time, and personnel in search of the perfect answer to an issue. Once you have decided on a direction, you may tend to isolate people who could derail it by offering alternatives. You might also tend to be overly reliant upon solutions that have worked in the past and the people who have executed those solutions.
insights: Invite creative thinkers into early decision-making sessions to include new ways of addressing things from the start.
Periodically meet with people involved and affected by the situation(s) to monitor their levels of buy-in.
Encourage open dialogue to surface concerns, questions, and ideas.
Clearly communicate decisions, especially those that will impact the work-lives of people.
Be prepared to re-group if the direction you have set is not embraced. Ask for suggestions to correct the concerns and adjust the direction if necessary.

CV.atBest: You will tend to get along with Connective Listeners and respect the “people” focus they bring to problem-solving. Connective listeners will tend to respect your measured approach that considers both facts and feelings when finding solutions.
CV.challenges: You may tune out or ignore Connective Listeners who begin to appeal to emotions or show themselves to be biased in their views. Connective Listeners may get frustrated when your approach seems too “procedural” or when you’re dwelling on the details in pursuit of the perfect solution to a problem.
CV.suggestions: Watch for the tendency to let problem-solving drag on because you’re looking for the perfect answer, and share your thinking early so Connective Listeners can feel included in the process. Outline clearly how your thinking will benefit or affect all the constituencies involved to help Connective Listeners see the value of your conclusions.

RV.atBest: You and Reflective Listeners will tend to appreciate each other’s expertise and respect your mutually deliberate style. You can bring an analytical eye and “systems view” to help refine Reflective Listeners’ thinking and move it forward toward practical solutions.
RV.challenges: You can reinforce each other’s tendency to spend too much time probing into problems before arriving at practical solutions. Together, you may get bogged down in “what is” while missing more creative possibilities of what could be.
RV.suggestions: Always give reflective listeners time and space to consider the issue at hand, and when you need input, try asking what their past experience tells them about the present issue. When brainstorming, keep the big picture in mind so you don’t get stuck in the weeds.

AL.atBest: You will tend to work well together, each respecting the other’s rational approach to problem solving. Together, you will set aside your biases and get right to the heart of an issue.
AL.challenges: When working together, you may tend to get bogged down in details, reinforcing each other’s perfectionism at the expense of the big picture. You may get frustrated with Analytical Listeners who don’t consider a solution’s impact on other people.
AL.suggestions: When brainstorming, provide yourselves with clearly defined priorities and outcomes with the big picture always in mind so you don’t get stuck in the weeds. Use facts and data to show a solution’s impact on other people or constituencies to help Analytical Listeners see the value of your thinking.

CL.atBest: Conceptual Listeners can offer fresh, creative ideas to supplement your “realist” thinking and help pull you out of the details when needed. You can help bridge the Conceptual Listener’s sense of possibility with structures and practical solutions that can bring their ideas into reality.
CL.challenges: Because you value what is factual, reliable, and known from experience, you may tune out Conceptual Listeners who creatively ideate on possibilities that you deem unrealistic at first glance. Conceptual Listeners may find your literal approach to be stifling and unimaginative.
CL.suggestions: While you may be tempted to ignore or play “devil’s advocate” with Conceptual Listeners, instead ask practical questions about how they might get from here to there. Value Conceptual Listeners as a helpful counter-balance that can offer creativity and imagination to your “head down” problem-solving approach.

name: The Inventor
intro: As an INVENTOR, you rely on Analytical Listening in combination with Reflective Listening and Conceptual Listening. You listen for everything that can be informative, creative, and of personal interest. You will tend to tune out when hammered for practicality and applicability of solutions.
shortDescription: Inventors rely on Analytical Listening in combination with Reflective Listening and Conceptual Listening. They listen for everything that can be informative, creative, and of personal interest. They will tend to tune out when hammered for practicality and applicability of solutions.
strengths: With your preference for Analytical, Reflective, and Conceptual Listening, you tend to strike a balance between deliberation and action in your decision-making process. You evaluate facts and data for their accuracy and usefulness, and are open to new ideas as long as they are practical and task-focused. You are not swayed by who is speaking, but more by the content being offered. You are also adept at applying previous knowledge and experiences to future-oriented thinking in order to move forward based on what did or did not work.
possibleChallenges: With your lower preference for Connective Listening, you may tend to overlook the impact decisions will have on other people and projects. You may also be more interested in your own experiences and knowledge than that of others. In short, you may sometimes come across as impersonal or unsympathetic. When listening to information, you might get caught between wanting more ideas and wanting more details. You might also get impatient when satisfied that sufficient information has been provided but others are continuing to deliberate.
insights: Develop and use questions that focus on others’ goals, wants, concerns, and ideas.
Take time to check with others for buy-in before moving to action.
Be aware that people’s feelings influence their ability and desire to perform to your standards.
Create decision-making criteria that go beyond your own personal knowledge. These might include: value of new ideas, amount of details needed, or impact on others.
Be patient when it seems that people are slowing down. They may need time to process, and their buy-in may pay off in the long run.

CV.atBest: Connective Listeners can help sell your ideas while making sure they take into consideration all the relevant constituencies that may be affected by them. You can think methodically through and analyze a Connective Listener’s inspirations and to help show what’s realistic and what should be jettisoned.
CV.challenges: Connective Listeners may perceive you as overly intellectual without showing enough interest in others. You may be unimpressed by Connective Listeners who bring a highly relational or emotional approach to interactions.
CV.suggestions: Outline clearly how your thinking will benefit or affect all the constituencies involved to help Connective Listeners see the value of your ideas and become advocates. Actively seek input from Connective Listeners to provide an important supplement to your otherwise practical, task-focused thinking.

RV.atBest: You will tend to appreciate the careful thinking Reflective Listeners contribute the situation at hand. You can bring an analytical eye as well as fresh ideas that help inspire Reflective Listeners to move their thinking forward.
RV.challenges: Reflective Listeners may get frustrated with your tendency to move quickly into ideation or problem- solving before they’ve had a chance to consider the issue fully. You may wonder why the Reflective Listener isn’t contributing their thoughts and ideas as readily as you.
RV.suggestions: Don’t expect Reflective Listeners to jump in as quickly as you; give them extra time to consider all the angles before offering their well-considered thoughts. As a way to engage them, ask Reflective Listeners about previous experiences they’ve had relevant to the current situation.

AL.atBest: You will tend to work well together, combining your respective rational and intellectual approaches to arrive at practical solutions to problems. Together, you can set aside your biases and focus on the facts as they pertain to the issue at hand.
AL.challenges: You may tune out when Analytical Listeners seem too detail oriented and aren’t contributing ideas that focus on the bigger picture. They may get frustrated when you begin ideating or ruminating around the issue instead of sticking more strictly to the hard data.
AL.suggestions: When speaking with Analytical Listeners, back up your ideas and lessons from previous experiences with relevant facts and data to help them see the value of your thinking. If Analytical Listeners start playing “devil’s advocate” with your ideas, welcome it as an opportunity to ferret out any weak points.

CL.atBest: Conceptual Listeners can help augment your thinking with fresh ideas and new, creative possibilities. You can help bridge the Conceptual Listener’s sense of possibility with structures and practical solutions that can bring their ideas into reality.
CL.challenges: While you value possibilities, you also value what is factual, reliable, and known from experience, so you may tune out Conceptual Listeners who creatively ideate on possibilities that you deem unrealistic at first glance. When you do ideate well together, you can still tend to leave out the “people” component and forget to consider how your ideas may impact other people, departments, or constituencies.
CL.suggestions: While you may be tempted to critically probe Conceptual Listeners’ ideas, instead ask practical questions about how they might get from here to there. When brainstorming with Conceptual Listeners, make a concerted effort to determine how your ideas would impact other potentially affected groups.

name: The Designer
intro: As a DESIGNER, you rely on Analytical Listening in combination with Conceptual Listening. You listen for fresh ways to deal with chronic as well as acute problems. You will tend to tune out when the discussion shifts toward unimaginative, tried-and-true options.
shortDescription: Designers rely on Analytical Listening in combination with Conceptual Listening. They listen for fresh ways to deal with chronic as well as acute problems. They will tend to tune out when the discussion shifts toward unimaginative, tried-and-true options.
strengths: With a high preference for Analytical and Conceptual Listening, you balance imaginative thinking with facts and data. You will dig below the surface to gain a deep understanding of the situation. You will then use familiarity with a problem to come up with new ideas to solve it. When idea- generating, you do not judge others, but you vet ideas for usefulness and practicality. You also offer fresh options and alternatives that might not have occurred to anyone before.
possibleChallenges: Your knack for creating solutions to problems could meander into over-thinking when there isn’t a concrete agenda or clearly defined problem. In this case, you may produce too many alternatives to execute. While you tend not to judge people, you don’t try to protect their feelings, either, and probably hold their ideas to a strict personal set of criteria unknown to them. You may at times get caught between logical and abstract thinking, which could freeze your forward movement. While you probably enjoy brainstorming, you are prone to engage it with a tight focus that produces narrow options rather than more open, creative ones.
insights: Balance the percentage of details offered with open-ended considerations. Both are needed.
Include facts and data during idea-generation, but try not to present them with a sense of indisputable authority.
Engage freely in the idea-generation process and evaluate ideas later.
Encourage others to generate ideas along with you.
Monitor your tendency to champion ideas just because they offer a different approach to a difficult situation.

CV.atBest: Your imaginative ideas and practical solutions can bring the Connective Listener’s people-oriented inspirations to reality. They can show you the different ways your ideas and proposed processes will affect various constituencies in ways you might not have considered.
CV.challenges: Because you value usefulness and practicality, you may tune out Connective Listeners who seem overly relational or emotionally biased in their thinking. Connective Listeners may get bored by your combination of logical and abstract thinking, and perceive you as aloof to the concerns of others.
CV.suggestions: Outline clearly how your thinking will benefit or affect all the constituencies involved to help Connective Listeners see the value of your ideas and become advocates. Resist the temptation to discard Connective Listeners who seem to soft or idealistic; they can bring a helpful “people” side to your practical thinking.

RV.atBest: You will tend to appreciate the personal knowledge and thoughtful perspectives Reflective Listeners bring to interactions. They will tend to appreciate your innovative thinking and will trust you to vet their ideas for feasibility.
RV.challenges: Reflective Listeners may tune out when you move too quickly into problem-solving or idea generating before they’ve had enough time to digest the full scope of the issue at hand. You may simply ignore the Reflective Listener who doesn’t seem to be engaging in outward ideating and problem solving.
RV.suggestions: Always give Reflective Listeners space and time to formulate their thoughts—they’ll bring useful insights that round out your thinking. To encourage their engagement, you might ask Reflective Listeners about previous experiences that would be relevant to the issue at hand.

AL.atBest: You will tend to work well together, sharing a mutual respect for facts, data, and practical methods of implementation without personal bias. Additionally, you will bring a slightly more “imaginative” side to the conversation and help Analytical Listeners find interesting new ways to apply their findings.
AL.challenges: Together, you can become narrowly focused and bogged down in the details of problem-solving without forward movement. You have the capacity to work diligently side-by-side without consulting each other and sharing the information needed to coordinate effectively.
AL.suggestions: When brainstorming, provide yourselves with clearly defined priorities and outcomes with a time-frame in mind so you don’t get stuck in the weeds. Regularly coordinate your efforts with each other to avoid blind spots and maximize your effectiveness.

CL.atBest: You will tend to work well together with your mutual appreciation for new ideas and possibilities. Additionally, you will bring a scrutinizing eye to the Conceptual Listener’s inspirations and help vet them for usefulness and practicality while keeping other constituencies in the back of your mind.
CL.challenges: While you’ll enjoy brainstorming with Conceptual Listeners, you probably won’t tolerate ideas that don’t have an obvious practical value. Even when ideating productively together, you will tend not to strongly consider how the ideas will affect other people.
CL.suggestions: Instead of tuning out or playing “devil’s advocate” with Conceptual Listeners whose ideas you deem unrealistic, ask them how they would get from here to there. When brainstorming with Conceptual Listeners, make a concerted effort to determine how your ideas would impact other potentially affected groups.

name: The Architect
intro: As an ARCHITECT, you rely on Analytical Listening in combination with Conceptual Listening and Connective Listening. You listen for details that will stimulate innovation. You will tend to tune out when nobody rises to the challenge of developing fresh ways to handle existing situations.
shortDescription: Architects rely on Analytical Listening in combination with Conceptual Listening and Connective Listening. They listen for details that will stimulate innovation. They will tend to tune out when nobody rises to the challenge of developing fresh ways to handle existing situations.
strengths: You are adept at considering both facts and options when thinking about the opportunity at hand. You also pay attention to the needs of people who will be affected by any given decision. With an agile mind, you develop more than one way to approach an issue, and you avoid using data to influence people in only one direction. You are open to multiple perspectives and will solicit others’ input since you trust in the knowledge and experience of those around you.
possibleChallenges: With a lower preference for Reflective Listening, you may tend to overlook some of the ways that your own knowledge and experience could apply to the opportunity at hand. You may not share your own vision of the issues or potentially innovative solutions. Your high Analytical Listening preference may lead you to ask for more details than the situation warrants. That, combined with your Conceptual Listening preference, could also lead you to be more interested in innovation in general than on what might actually work. You also may tend to vacillate between seeking facts and wanting new ideas, which can impede collaboration and decision-making.
insights: Share your thinking and feelings with others to increase engagement.
Ask yourself, “What do I know that we can use to resolve these issues?”
Assess the relevance of information gained from others, and how it will add value to possible solutions.
While you may be tempted to develop innovations quickly, take time to make sure they are based in practicality.
Check your vision for success to make sure it is in line with those who will implement your ideas.

CV.atBest: You will tend to respect the openness and “people focus” Connective Listeners bring to interactions, and they can help you ensure your ideas have factored in all constituencies. You can offer imaginative ideas and practical solutions that will help Connective Listeners bring their people-oriented inspirations into reality.
CV.challenges: Because you value usefulness and practicality, you may tune out Connective Listeners who seem overly relational or emotionally biased in their thinking. Connective Listeners may tune out when your interest in logical and abstract thinking takes over for extended periods.
CV.suggestions: Outline clearly how your thinking will benefit or affect all the constituencies involved to help Connective Listeners see the value of your ideas and become advocates. Resist the temptation to discard Connective Listeners who seem too soft or idealistic; they can help ensure you’ve considered the needs of everyone potentially affected by your ideas.

RV.atBest: You will tend to appreciate the personal knowledge and thoughtful perspectives Reflective Listeners bring to interactions. They will appreciate your innovative thinking and will trust you to vet their ideas for feasibility.
RV.challenges: Reflective Listeners may tune out when you move too quickly into problem-solving or idea generating before they’ve had enough time to digest the full scope of the issue at hand. You may feel impatient or uncomfortable when a Reflective Listener doesn’t seem to be outwardly engaging in the unfolding problem-solving or ideation.
