RV.suggestions: Always give Reflective Listeners space and time to formulate their thoughts—they’ll bring useful insights that round out your thinking. To encourage their engagement, you might ask Reflective Listeners about previous experiences that would be relevant to the issue at hand.

AL.atBest: You will tend to work well together, sharing a mutual respect for facts, data, and practical methods of implementation. Additionally, you will bring a slightly more “imaginative” side to the conversation and help Analytical Listeners find interesting new ways to apply their findings and consider how they might affect others.
AL.challenges: When working together, you might become bogged down in the details of problem-solving without enough forward movement. You also have the potential to become frustrated with Analytical Listeners who aren’t thinking “creatively” enough or taking others into consideration.
AL.suggestions: When brainstorming, provide yourselves with clearly defined priorities and outcomes with a time-frame in mind so you don’t get pulled unnecessarily into the weeds. Since it likely won’t be a first impulse for either of you, try to make a conscious effort to stop and consider how lessons from previous experiences could inform the current issue you’re addressing.

CL.atBest: You will tend to work well together with your mutual appreciation for new ideas and possibilities. You will bring a scrutinizing eye to the Conceptual Listener's inspirations and help vet them for usefulness and practicality while keeping other constituencies in the back of your mind.
CL.challenges: While you’ll enjoy brainstorming with Conceptual Listeners, you probably won’t tolerate ideas that aren’t grounded in facts and will be tempted to play “devils advocate” with ideas you deem unrealistic at first glance. Together, you have the potential to get carried away with concepts and ideas that may be backed by logic and facts, but aren’t necessarily grounded in practical experience.
CL.suggestions: Instead of tuning out or playing “devil’s advocate” with Conceptual Listeners whose ideas aren’t backed by logic, ask them how they would get from here to there. When brainstorming with Conceptual Listeners, you’ll naturally make sure ideas are backed by facts, but make a concerted effort to also weigh ideas against the knowledge you’ve gained through practical experience.

name: The Measurer
intro: As a MEASURER, you rely on Analytical Listening in combination with Conceptual Listening and Reflective Listening. You listen for details that will advance a situation along a sensible course. You will tend to tune out people who seemingly want to derail the process.
shortDescription: Measurers rely on Analytical Listening in combination with Conceptual Listening and Reflective Listening. They listen for details that will advance a situation along a sensible course. They will tend to tune out people who seemingly want to derail the process.
strengths: You appreciate the role of both linear and abstract thought in creating direction. In addition, you will tend to bring your past knowledge and experience to bear on an issue. You weigh the validity of information being presented against your own expertise, and pay attention to facts along with your own intuition during an interaction. With a measured eye, you are not easily swayed by others’ feelings or ungrounded opinions.
possibleChallenges: You tend to process ideas internally and may not pick up on all the nuances of information being shared. Due to your low preference for Connective Listening, you may not consider how to best convey to an audience content or possibilities that you are fascinated by. Due to your high preference for Analytical Listening, you may overlook feelings and grow impatient with people who do not speak or present ideas supported by facts and data. Once a direction has been set, you are unlikely to openly hear out people who advocate ideas that do not fit that direction.
insights: You might think, “I’ve got it” when you really don’t. Be patient.
Listen fully until others have completed speaking and do not interrupt them.
Ask questions as a technique to engage people who do not think or listen like you.
Be an active participant, not an observer of an interaction, and display your body language to signal you are engaged.
If people try to highjack your direction, stop the process by asking questions about where they are headed and what they propose.

CV.atBest: You will offer practical, measured thinking and imagination that can help bring the Connective Listener’s people-oriented inspirations into reality. Connective Listeners can show you the different ways your ideas and proposed processes will affect various constituencies beyond what you might have considered.
CV.challenges: Because you value usefulness and practicality, you may tune out Connective Listeners who seem overly relational or emotionally biased in their thinking. Connective Listeners, in turn, may get bored by your combination of logical and abstract thinking, and perceive you as aloof to the concerns of others.
CV.suggestions: Outline clearly how your thinking will benefit or affect all the constituencies involved to help Connective Listeners see the value of your ideas and become advocates. Resist the temptation to discard Connective Listeners who seem too soft or idealistic; they can bring a helpful “people” side to your practical thinking and help sell your ideas to others.

RV.atBest: You will tend to appreciate the personal knowledge and thoughtful perspectives Reflective Listeners bring to interactions. They will tend to appreciate your innovative thinking and will trust you to vet their ideas for feasibility.
RV.challenges: Reflective Listeners may tune out when you move too quickly into problem-solving or idea generating before they’ve had enough time to digest the full scope of the issue at hand. You may simply ignore the Reflective Listener who doesn’t seem to be engaging in outward ideating and problem solving as they are considering ideas internally.
RV.suggestions: Always give Reflective Listeners space and time to formulate their thoughts—they’ll bring useful insights that round out your thinking. To encourage their engagement, you might ask Reflective Listeners about previous experiences that would be relevant to the issue at hand.

AL.atBest: You will tend to work well together, sharing a mutual respect for facts, data, and practical methods of implementation. You will bring a slightly more “imaginative” side to the conversation with thoughtful considerations that will help Analytical Listeners find interesting new ways to apply their findings.
AL.challenges: Together, you can become narrowly focused and bogged down in the details of problem-solving without significant forward movement. You also have the capacity to work diligently side-by-side without consulting each other and sharing the information needed to coordinate effectively.
AL.suggestions: When brainstorming, provide yourselves with clearly defined priorities and outcomes with a time-frame in mind so you don’t get stuck in the weeds. Regularly communicate your efforts with each other to make sure you’re still on the “same page” and coordinating effectively.

CL.atBest: You will tend to work well together with your mutual appreciation for new ideas and possibilities. You will bring a scrutinizing eye to the Conceptual Listener’s inspirations and help vet them for usefulness and practicality.
CL.challenges: While you’ll enjoy brainstorming with Conceptual Listeners, you probably won’t tolerate ideas that contradict what you already know to be true from experience, or that don’t have an obvious practical value, and may take them down through critical questioning. Even when brainstorming productively together, you may tend to mutually ignore how the ideas will affect other people.
CL.suggestions: Instead of tuning out or playing “devil’s advocate” with Conceptual Listeners whose ideas you deem unrealistic, ask them how they would get from “here” to “there” and see what ideas they come up with. When brainstorming with Conceptual Listeners, make a concerted effort to determine how your ideas would impact other potentially affected groups. CONCEPTUAL LISTENING PROFILES

name: The Idea-Generator
intro: As an IDEA-GENERATOR, you prefer Conceptual Listening most. You listen for new, interesting, and different ideas and options. You will tend to tune out when there is no tolerance or room for fresh thinking.
shortDescription: Idea-Generators prefer Conceptual Listening most. They listen for new, interesting, and different ideas and options. They will tend to tune out when there is no tolerance or room for fresh thinking.
strengths: You listen for new and different ideas, and are able to provide fresh insights to interactions. Your range of thinking is not bound by the parameters others might take for granted, so you appreciate a broad application of information beyond the scope of the issue at hand. You tend to be possibilities-focused, not constrained by “what is” at present. Your wide range of thinking also gives you a knack for connecting disparate ideas into something more robust than they were individually.
possibleChallenges: If a meeting or presentation isn’t stimulating to you, you may be inclined to shut down prematurely. Because you have an appetite for new ideas and continued possibilities, you might have a hard time feeling settled with available information; instead, you’d prefer more options, ideas and possibilities. You may want to keep developing ideas, even when it’s time to move on. Because you value interesting ideas more than details and minutiae, you may also forget to look for where and how to apply ideas that have been developed.
insights: Stay focused on what the speaker is saying without going off into the realm of possibilities.
Ask yourself what critical factors should be understood before new options and alternatives are considered.
Take time to reflect on what others are suggesting before rushing to idea-generation.
Ask fact-based and detailed questions that show interest in rational thinking.
Observe the reactions of others and modulate yourself if you’re sensing your enthusiasm and pace are turning them off.

CV.atBest: You can help Connective Listeners take their people-oriented ideas to imaginative levels they hadn’t considered. They will offer you room to be yourself and ideate, while making sure your ideas are taking all possible constituencies into consideration.
CV.challenges: You may share a good “brainstorming” rapport but it might not have the consideration of resources needed to actually follow through on the ideas generated. Alternatively, you may find a tension growing when the Connective Listener’s interest in people and processes conflicts with your interest in ideas and possibilities.
CV.suggestions: Try to keep an agenda to the conversation that has a structure and clearly defined goals to curb the possibility of drifting into prolonged ideating. While you'll naturally speak of ideas and how they might impact people, try also to round out your brainstorming with the reality of facts, data, and lessons from previous experiences wherever possible.

RV.atBest: Reflective Listeners will listen to your ideas and offer a practical check based on the knowledge and experience they bring to bear on the issue. You can offer Reflective Listeners fresh ideas and possibilities they can add to their thinking.
RV.challenges: You may grow frustrated with the Reflective Listener’s tendency to “hold back” and not speak until they’ve fully considered everything. They may become frustrated with your ability to ideate at length and conceptually entertain different possibilities to see where they might lead.
RV.suggestions: Support your ideas with case studies and examples to get Reflective Listeners on the same page with you. When starting to brainstorm, ask Reflective Listeners if they've had previous experiences relevant to the topic at hand as a way to garner their early participation.

AL.atBest: You can offer a sense of imagination and fresh direction to balance the Analytical Listener’s “literalness”, especially when they become stuck in details. Analytical Listeners can effectively vet your ideas and inspirations to determine what is realistic and what isn’t.
AL.challenges: Because Analytical Listeners highly value what is true, factual, and measurable, they may become frustrated with your proclivity to ideate on possibilities. You may find Analytical Listeners to be stifling and unimaginative, especially when they start probing your ideas to see how they hold up.
AL.suggestions: Try to value the Analytical Listener’s probing questions as a helpful grounding mechanism to ensure your ideas are thoroughly vetted. When ideating with Analytical Listeners, make sure to also consider how your ideas may affect other people or stakeholders of any kind.

CL.atBest: You will naturally enjoy brainstorming together and will therefore feel like you’re on the same wavelength. Together, you can foster mutual creativity and ideation with almost perpetual stamina and enthusiasm.
CL.challenges: If you aren’t careful, you can stay in continuous “ideating” mode without arriving at practical solutions. You can reinforce each other’s excitement about new possible directions while mutually ignoring the feasibility of their implementation.
CL.suggestions: When brainstorming, provide clearly defined priorities and outcomes which can serve as a framework within which to better focus thinking & ideation. Try to incorporate facts & data and regularly consider how your ideas might affect other people or departments.

name: The Brainstormer
intro: As a BRAINSTORMER, you rely on Conceptual Listening in combination with Connective Listening. You listen for ideas that can be shared with others. You will tend to tune out when there is no room for discussion about options and how they might be used.
shortDescription: Brainstormers rely on Conceptual Listening in combination with Connective Listening. They listen for ideas that can be shared with others. They will tend to tune out when there is no room for discussion about options and how they might be used.
strengths: Because you are highly Conceptual and Connective in your listening, you have a knack for generating possible options and alternatives from the wide array of knowledge and experiences held by others. When considering new ideas or alternatives, you are empathetic to the concerns of others; you think creatively about new ideas and their relevance to other people and departments. You combine the question of “if” with the question of “who”, and no idea is too big if others buy in. You tune into group member’s feelings during idea-generation, and are adept at making sure there is buy-in before moving forward.
possibleChallenges: Facts and details are not your first love, so you may sacrifice them in favor of big-picture possibilities. You can be very enthusiastic and occasionally oversell your ideas before thinking the details through. You are eager to move into action, and can grow impatient with others who want more time for deliberation, even if the strategy is not yet clear. When you do move into action, you still may not stop coming up with new ideas, which can make it difficult to stick with one plan. If there isn’t a “people” component to ideas being discussed, you may simply ignore them or filter them out.
insights: Balance how much and how often you talk in comparison to the rest of the group. Too much enthusiasm can overwhelm some listeners.
Pace the presentation of your ideas so others have a chance to absorb each one before you go on to the next.
Use body language, voice and eye contact to show others you are connecting to them. Look at contributors to show you are interested in them as well as their ideas.
Do not allow impatience with the process to get in the way of providing your ideas.
If there isn’t time for all your ideas to be heard, jot them down and share them later.

CV.atBest: You will create a welcoming environment that inspires Connective Listeners to bolster their “people- focus” with a sense of fresh creativity and possibility. Connective Listeners will be strong allies and supporters of your inspirations which tend to consider all relevant constituencies.
CV.challenges: Together, you may enjoy brainstorming for the social aspects without paying enough attention to the facts and details required to move forward with practical solutions. You may wrongly assume that because of mutual enthusiasm and similarity in thinking, you must be on the right track.
CV.suggestions: Remember that mutual enthusiasm is not enough; try to ground your conversations in facts, data, and knowledge you each bring from previous experiences. Try to keep an agenda to the conversation that has a structure and clearly defined goals to curb the possibility of drifting into prolonged ideating.

RV.atBest: You will offer Reflective Listeners a welcoming space to contribute their thoughts, knowledge, and experience. Reflective Listeners can take in your ideas and offer back insights based on their own knowledge and expertise.
RV.challenges: Because you tend to appreciate input from everybody during idea-generation, you may become uncomfortable with Reflective Listeners who are slow to offer their contributions. Reflective Listeners may tune out when the pace of your brainstorming doesn’t allow enough space to consider deeply all the angles.
RV.suggestions: Always give Reflective Listeners plenty of time and room to process new information before having to offer back their input. When you need to solicit their input, try asking Reflective Listeners about previous experiences they’ve had relevant to the issue under consideration.

AL.atBest: Analytical Listeners can help vet your ideas based on agreed-upon criteria and move your inspirations toward practical implementation. You can offer a sense of imagination and possible direction to balance the Analytical Listener’s “literalness”, especially when they become stuck in details.
AL.challenges: You may tune out Analytical Listeners when they fail to incorporate either an imaginative or human component into their solutions. Analytical Listeners may stop listening seriously to your ideation—or begin to ask probing questions— when you don’t offer enough practical rationale to back up your thinking.
AL.suggestions: Try to value the Analytical Listener’s probing questions as a helpful grounding mechanism to ensure your ideas are thoroughly vetted. While you habitually tend to speak from ideas and emotions, try to incorporate more facts and data when speaking to Analytical Listeners to help them see the importance of your thinking.

CL.atBest: You will naturally enjoy brainstorming together and will likely feel that you’re on the same wavelength. You will make sure those outside the immediate conversation—the rest of the team, the company, and other constituents—are included within the scope of your brainstorming.
CL.challenges: You can fall in love with the brainstorming process that you both excel at, thus leaving out others who might have ideas to contribute, but aren’t as practiced and skilled as you. In your naturally comfortable brainstorming rapport, you may reinforce each other’s excitement about new possible directions while mutually ignoring the feasibility of their implementation.
CL.suggestions: When brainstorming, provide yourselves with clearly defined priorities and outcomes which can serve as a framework within which to better focus thinking & ideation. While you'll naturally speak of ideas and how they might impact people, try also to round out your brainstorming with facts, data, and lessons from previous experiences wherever possible.

name: The Ally
intro: As an ALLY, you rely on Conceptual Listening in combination with Connective Listening and Reflective Listening. You listen with an ear to benefit situations by offering ideas derived from your knowledge and experience. You will tend to tune out if the ideas you offer are ignored.
shortDescription: Allys rely on Conceptual Listening in combination with Connective Listening and Reflective Listening. They listen with an ear to benefit situations by offering ideas derived from their knowledge and experience. They will tend to tune out if the ideas they offer are ignored.
strengths: You pay equal attention to people and ideas without being critical, and balance your personal experience with the experience and input of others. You can make decisions quickly and with confidence, while considering how options can help other employees as well as external customers. When dealing with people issues, you are open to examining the merits of alternative approaches.
possibleChallenges: You tend to be more interested in what “could be” rather than what is known, and therefore quick to move ahead without gathering all the relevant facts and details of an issue. You may also have a tendency to be personally carried away by your own ideas, or persuaded by people with strong opinions and charismatic personalities. On projects, you may vacillate between wanting to collaborate with others and going it alone, which can confuse others involved.
insights: Develop criteria to objectively evaluate ideas before making decisions.
Ask open questions to invite a broadened examination of an issue.
Be aware of your growing impatience and let others know when you are losing attention.
Take notes or doodle to maintain your attention.
Don’t take it personally when people ask you for more details before they buy in.

CV.atBest: Since you both share a relational component, you’ll tend to get along easily, each incorporating the other’s thinking into your decision-making. Additionally, you will bring fresh ideas to the interaction and speak from experience that the Connective Listener will appreciate.
CV.challenges: Together, you may enjoy brainstorming for the feeling of connection without paying enough attention to the facts and details required to move forward with practical solutions. You may be swayed by each other and prematurely assume that because of mutual enthusiasm and similarity in thinking, you must be on the right track.
CV.suggestions: Remember that mutual enthusiasm is not enough; try to ground your conversations in facts and data to ensure your thinking is practical. Try to keep an agenda to the conversation that has a structure, timeline, and clearly defined goals to curb the possibility of drifting into prolonged ideating.

RV.atBest: You will offer a welcoming atmosphere that affords Reflective Listeners time and support for their input. Reflective Listeners can bolster and deepen your own ideas with helpful insights from their knowledge and expertise.
RV.challenges: The Reflective Listener may get less of a thrill from ideating than you do, and may tune out when your social side starts distracting from the task at hand. You might be easily swayed by Reflective Listeners who show impressive depth and expertise in a given field or subject.
RV.suggestions: When collaborating, provide yourselves with clearly defined priorities and outcomes and make an effort to support your mutual thinking with relevant facts and details. If you find yourself too often deferring to their expertise, use those moments as reminders to pause and think back to your own depths of knowledge.

AL.atBest: Analytical Listeners can help vet your ideas based on available resources and move your inspirations toward practical implementation. You can offer a sense of imagination and possible direction to balance the Analytical Listener’s “literalness”, especially when they become stuck in details.
AL.challenges: You may tune out Analytical Listeners when they fail to incorporate either an imaginative or human component into their solutions. Analytical Listeners may stop listening seriously to your ideation—or may begin to ask probing questions— when you don’t offer enough practical rationale to back up your thinking.
AL.suggestions: Try to value the Analytical Listener’s probing questions as a helpful grounding mechanism to ensure your ideas are thoroughly vetted. While you habitually tend to speak from ideas and emotions, try to incorporate more facts and data when speaking to Analytical Listeners. If you do, they will be more likely to see the importance of your thinking.

CL.atBest: You and Conceptual Listeners will naturally enjoy brainstorming together and will likely feel that you’re on the same wavelength. You will also help ground the ideas in consideration for others while drawing upon what has and hasn’t worked in the past.
CL.challenges: If you aren’t careful, you can stay in continuous “ideating” mode without arriving at practical solutions. In your naturally comfortable brainstorming rapport, you may reinforce each other’s excitement about new possible directions while mutually ignoring relevant facts, data, and resource considerations.
CL.suggestions: When brainstorming, provide yourselves with clearly defined priorities and outcomes which can serve as a framework within which to better focus thinking & ideation. While you'll naturally speak of new ideas and how they might positively impact people, try also to round out your brainstorming by intentionally looking for problem areas—such as conflicting facts or resource considerations—that may help ground your thinking.

name: The Applier
intro: As an APPLIER, you rely on Conceptual Listening in combination with Connective Listening and Analytical Listening. You listen for ideas that others can use to solve problems. You will tend to tune out if there are no practical ideas suggested.
shortDescription: Appliers rely on Conceptual Listening in combination with Connective Listening and Analytical Listening. They listen for ideas that others can use to solve problems. They will tend to tune out if there are no practical ideas suggested.
strengths: You have an ability to contribute new and practical ideas to a conversation while taking the facts into consideration. You are also sensitive to others’ feelings behind interactions. When problem solving, you look for possibilities and options while considering how any decision will affect others. You balance details with big-picture thinking and tend not to impose your personal agenda on those around you.
possibleChallenges: While considering new ideas, basing them in facts, and thinking of their impact on others, you may tend to overlook how you yourself might utilize information that is being presented. You also might not fully analyze what is already known about the situation under discussion and what more is required to arrive at good solutions. You have a strong bias for action which can lead you to be impatient with reflective people or cast aside people who are not on board. You may sometimes be overly influenced by outside factors such as experts, newest ideas, and/or competition.
insights: Filter ideas and details by what is already known and by what you have experienced.
Listen equally for both facts and feelings.
Balance your time to think and reflect with your desire to move forward.
Trust your own expertise to inform you about what is important to pay attention to.
Listen to evaluate information, but not with the intention to criticize.

CV.atBest: Connective Listeners will provide a welcoming platform to test the usefulness of theories, ideas, and tools that you contribute. You provide Connective Listeners with creative and well-reasoned options that they will tend to appreciate and use.
CV.challenges: Together, you may enjoy forward movement in brainstorming without grounding the conversation enough in your personal knowledge or lessons gained from past experience. You may intrigue Connective Listeners and prematurely assume that because of mutual enthusiasm and agreement in thinking, you must be on the right track.
CV.suggestions: Bring a clear set of goals to your brainstorming that lists what you need to know before making any final decisions; resist the temptation to move into action too quickly. Make an effort to look inward from time-to-time when brainstorming to see how your own knowledge and experience could add to the situation.

RV.atBest: A Reflective Listener will be a grounding partner to your thinking with helpful insights based on their knowledge and expertise. You can help Reflective Listeners move forward creatively with their own ideas.
RV.challenges: You may be tempted to move quickly into action and thus ignore Reflective Listeners who want more time to study / think about the situation. Reflective listeners may become frustrated with your tendency to move ahead without suﬃcient deliberation.
RV.suggestions: Always give Reflective Listeners plenty of time to process incoming information before asking them to offer their input. When starting to brainstorm, ask Reflective Listeners if they've had previous experiences relevant to the topic at hand as a way to garner their early participation.

AL.atBest: You will appreciate the Analytical Listener’s ability to add concrete data to inform your decisions and inspirations. The Analytical Listener will appreciate your tendency to offer ideas that are focused and structured.
AL.challenges: You may tune out Analytical Listeners when they fail to incorporate either an imaginative or human component into their solutions. They may question your forward movement, especially if you’re moving pre-emptively without enough consideration of the data.
AL.suggestions: Welcome an Analytical Listener’s critical questions, especially if you find yourself moving forward on limited information or due to others’ influence. To help Analytical Listeners understand the value of your thinking, communicate your ideas and inspirations using as much factual and data-driven information as possible.

CL.atBest: You will naturally enjoy brainstorming together and will likely feel that you’re on the same wavelength. You will make sure those outside the immediate conversation—the rest of the team, the company, and other constituents—are included within the scope of your brainstorming.
CL.challenges: You may prematurely assume that because of your natural brainstorming rapport, you’re automatically on the right track. If you aren’t careful, you can stay in continuous “ideating” mode and forget to tap into your own relevant knowledge based on past experience.
CL.suggestions: When brainstorming, provide yourselves with clearly defined priorities and outcomes which can serve as a framework within which to better focus thinking & ideation. While you'll naturally incorporate available data with new ideas to see how they might positively impact people, try also to round out your brainstorming by intentionally looking for ways your previous lessons and experiences could add value.

name: The Contemplator
intro: As a CONTEMPLATOR, you rely on Conceptual Listening in combination with Reflective Listening. You listen for new ideas that can supplement what you know or have experienced. You will tend to tune out if not given enough time to think about how the information can add personal value.
shortDescription: Contemplators rely on Conceptual Listening in combination with Reflective Listening. They listen for new ideas that can supplement what they know or have experienced. They will tend to tune out if not given enough time to think about how the information can add personal value.
strengths: You tend to be a deep listener who reflects before offering your own ideas. You are particularly good at developing useful ideas to help solve issues at work, especially when they have personal significance to you. You are independent, and can be left alone to think through options and possibilities, then generate fresh ideas. You’re content being on your own, and have no high need to control others or be controlled.
possibleChallenges: Because you have a high preference for Reflective and Conceptual Listening, you are prone to get “stuck in your own head.” You may hold your own ideas with high regard without necessarily giving others their due consideration. You may also overlook facts and data that could prove relevant to your ideas. On certain occasions, you may hold back your ideas entirely, neglecting to share them with others, no matter how valuable. Because you value time to think information through, you may be impatient with people who quickly probe you for answers.
insights: If you tend to hold back in meetings, make sure to speak up and share your ideas.
Take notes and doodle to keep focused and interested.
Engage throughout a presentation to demonstrate you are thinking and paying attention.
Ask conceptual questions to bring the interaction around to your sphere of interest. For example, “What if we scrapped all our previous knowledge and started from the beginning?”
Search for details and facts that might add valuable insight, and that might have been overlooked.

CV.atBest: You may not always be quick to share ideas, but you will likely find it natural to share your ideas with Connective Listeners, and they will find it natural to listen, given their relational nature. Connective Listeners can take your ideas and show how they might affect other relevant people, customers, or processes in ways you may not have considered.
CV.challenges: Your penchant for carefully considering ideas before speaking and their desire to engage more immediately can create a tension that the Connective Listeners will naturally take the responsibility for smoothing over. You enjoy ideating, but Connective Listeners may not share the same enthusiasm unless the ideas have immediate relevance to—or benefit for—others.
CV.suggestions: When you’re not ready yet to share ideas, don’t hesitate to let Connective Listeners know you need more time to consider the issue at hand. They’ll respect this. Frame your ideas in a way that highlights their effect on other people to help Connective Listeners understand the value of your thinking.

RV.atBest: Together, you share a mutual appreciation for knowledge and understanding and can explore overlapping areas of expertise at length. With your mutually reserved style, you’ll respect each other’s time to process your thinking before speaking.
RV.challenges: You may be tempted to spend too much time probing interesting subjects or areas of expertise rather than moving forward with the task-at-hand. Without checking with one another, you each could preemptively assume that because of similarity in thinking you’re on the right track.
RV.suggestions: When brainstorming, provide yourselves with clearly defined priorities and outcomes which can serve as a framework within which to better focus thinking & ideation. Before you get too far, make sure you are sharing your ideas and thinking along the way.

AL.atBest: You can offer an Analytical Listener fresh ideas and different perspectives collected from your own experience. They can help serve as a grounding force for your ideas, often by asking probing questions to see where they hold up.
AL.challenges: Analytical Listeners may feel impatient with your ideas if you don’t ground them in facts, data, or practicality. You may feel annoyed by their literal approach and what you perceive as a lack of imagination.
AL.suggestions: Try not to take the Analytical Listener’s probing as a statement of personal mistrust. Rather, value them as a counterweight that brings balance to your thinking. When speaking to Analytical Listeners, support your ideas with facts, data, and practical details to help them understand the importance of your thinking.

CL.atBest: You will tend to naturally share your thoughts with Conceptual Listeners who, with their enthusiasm for ideation, will draw you into collaboration. You can help slow the Conceptual Listener’s temptation to go on and on with ideas by grounding them through your lens of experience to see which ideas might work in the situation.
CL.challenges: Your greatest mutual strength could become your mutual handicap when time is short and you’re unable to translate your thinking into a quick decision. You might get “stuck in the clouds” together, constantly considering new angles on an issue, without enough grounding in real-time facts, data, or resource considerations.
CL.suggestions: When brainstorming, provide yourselves with a clear list of priorities and a timeframe that will force you to “reign in” your thinking. With your preference for Reflective Listening, you can further help ground conversations by speaking from your own experience of what has and has not worked in the past.

name: The Translator
intro: As a TRANSLATOR, you rely on Conceptual Listening in combination with Reflective Listening and Connective Listening. You listen for ways to improve situations for others. You will tend to tune out if options that people will find of value are not surfaced or made available.
shortDescription: Translators rely on Conceptual Listening in combination with Reflective Listening and Connective Listening. They listen for ways to improve situations for others. They will tend to tune out if options that people will find of value are not surfaced or made available.
strengths: You are able to collaborate with a broad spectrum of people to develop fresh ideas for both chronic and acute problems. In doing so, you help create an environment that encourages people to try options that might be a stretch. Additionally, you are adept at translating abstract ideas for others to grasp. When listening to new ideas, you filter them through your own previous experience and knowledge to assess their value. You are both empathetic to others and self- reflective, which increases authentic understanding.
possibleChallenges: You may tend to overlook details and facts in favor of creative, appealing ideas. You can also be co-opted by who is speaking, rather than what is being said. If the information being shared is not stimulating to you, or doesn’t include a human element, you may tend to stop listening. You’re most likely to lose patience when presented with endless details.
insights: Develop questions that require you to gather facts that pertain to the issues at hand.
Objectively examine the information being presented separate from who is presenting it.
Write down ideas as they occur and review them after an interaction.
Populate a map of stakeholders and ask, “Who will be involved and affected by this information?”
Notice your level of patience and when there is a feeling of drifting. Refocus by moving around, or taking notes.

CV.atBest: You will tend to get along well with Connective Listeners and respect the “people” focus they bring to problem-solving. Connective listeners will tend to respect your ability to consider multiple points of view and translate abstract concepts for others to grasp.
CV.challenges: You may tend to tune out or ignore Connective Listeners if they become biased in their views or appeal too strongly to emotions. Connective Listeners may get frustrated if you remain too long in abstract thinking or ideating without collecting further information from others involved.
CV.suggestions: Value Connective Listeners as a helpful resource that will signal whether you’re ideating too much without considering all the interested parties. When brainstorming with Connective Listeners, try to ground your thoughts in facts and available data to mitigate the possibility of “ungrounded” ideating together.

RV.atBest: You and Reflective Listeners will tend to appreciate each other’s expertise and respect your mutually thoughtful style. You can bring fresh ideas and a “systems view” to help bring further insight to other Reflective Listeners’ thinking.
RV.challenges: You may enjoy tossing out ideas at a faster rate than most Reflective Listeners, who may start to tune you out if they don’t have time to process all the angles. You could simply “pass over” Reflective Listeners who aren’t speaking up and adding to the ideas and options under consideration.
RV.suggestions: Always give Reflective Listeners plenty of time and space to digest and process their thinking; the wait is usually worth it. If you need them to engage, ask Reflective Listeners if they’ve had previous experiences that might help inform the issue at hand.

AL.atBest: Analytical Listeners can help balance your preference for knowledge gathering and idea generating with an eye for relevant facts, data, and practical resources. You, in turn, can take an Analytical Listener’s practical findings, incorporate them with current needs, and find creative ways to move forward.
AL.challenges: Because you enjoy moving forward and testing out new ideas that seem reasonable based on your previous experiences, you may get frustrated with Analytical Listeners who seem overly literal in their approach or stuck in the details. They, in turn, may question the validity of some of your ideas and possibly probe them for signs of weakness.
AL.suggestions: Try to value the Analytical Listener’s “devil’s advocate” approach as an important way to sharpen your thinking and make sure all relevant facts have been taken into consideration. When speaking to Analytical Listeners, support your ideas with relevant facts, data, or other statistics to help them understand the value of your thinking.

CL.atBest: You’ll appreciate the ideation of Conceptual Listeners while checking their ideas against your own experience and considering their relevance for others. Conceptual Listeners will help bolster your own thinking with new angles and possibilities that even you hadn’t considered.
CL.challenges: Together, you may share a good “brainstorming” rapport but it may also lack grounding in facts, details, and a discerning eye for what’s practical given the situation and resources. You may tend to be swayed by charismatic Conceptual Listeners, especially when they back themselves up with imaginative and convincing ideas.
CL.suggestions: When brainstorming, provide yourselves with clearly defined priorities and outcomes and bring facts and data to support your ideas. When ideating with Conceptual Listeners, make a point to “tap into” your Reflective Listening side to consider what could work based on your previous experiences, thereby limiting the possibility of getting “pulled into the clouds” together.

name: The Pragmatist
intro: As a PRAGMATIST, you rely on Conceptual Listening in combination with Reflective Listening and Analytical Listening. You listen with an open, yet critical ear. You will tend to tune out when ideas that have been proposed are not practical.
shortDescription: Pragmatists rely on Conceptual Listening in combination with Reflective Listening and Analytical Listening. They listen with an open, yet critical ear. They will tend to tune out when ideas that have been proposed are not practical.
strengths: When listening, you search for ideas that are relevant to the issues under consideration, and are not influenced by the personality of the speaker. You have an appreciation for creativity, especially when it offers a practical real-world component. You pay attention to facts and data, and are not easily swayed by rhetoric or false claims. You understand complex issues and get to the bottom of them, often bringing fresh perspectives along the way.
possibleChallenges: You think a lot, and may grow impatient when asked to verbalize everything you’ve just considered internally. While you are a creative and practical thinker, you may sometimes overlook the opinions of other people, departments, or important relationships. This could lead you to defending your ideas when others question them, and you may feel like you’re being drilled even when this isn’t the questioner’s intention. Finally, because you listen for facts over feelings, you may sometimes miss the emotional aspects of an interaction.
insights: Learn about the people you will be listening to before an interaction.
Develop questions that will surface their interests, concerns, problems, etc.
Enter an interaction with the intention to learn rather than to tell or display your knowledge.
Share your own ideas and thoughts rather than keeping them in your head.
Watch for clues into other people’s feelings, and acknowledge and respond to them.

CV.atBest: Connective Listeners will tend to appreciate the fresh perspectives and practical thinking you bring to the issues at hand. You can appreciate their ability to show how your ideas might affect other relevant people, customers, or processes in ways you hadn’t considered.
CV.challenges: You may tend not to speak much in meetings, which can concern Connective Listeners, and when you do, your perspective may not strongly consider the opinions or needs of other constituents, which may lead Connective Listeners to disagree with your ideas. You may tune out Connective Listeners when they seem too emotionally invested in a topic, eager to have everyone contribute, or overly concerned with others’ needs at the expense of more pragmatic considerations.
CV.suggestions: Try to regularly share what you’re thinking, or the considerations you’re having, even if you haven’t arrived at a conclusion. Consider how your solutions would affect other people and constituencies in order to help Connective Listeners see the value of your thinking.

RV.atBest: Together, you share a mutual appreciation for knowledge and understanding and can explore overlapping areas of expertise at length. You’ll easily relate to the more reserved style of the Reflective Listener, which they’ll appreciate, and you can also offer fresh perspectives and practical considerations to their thinking.
RV.challenges: You may be tempted to spend too much time probing interesting subjects or questions rather than moving forward with the task-at-hand. You each could assume that because of similarity in thinking you’re on the right track.
RV.suggestions: When brainstorming, provide yourselves with clearly defined priorities and outcomes and bring facts and data to support your ideas. Before you get too far, ask yourself and each other how your ideas might impact other stakeholders— team members, clients, business partners, etc.

AL.atBest: Analytical Listeners will tend to appreciate your balanced approach with your ability to set emotions aside to arrive at pragmatic conclusions. You will generally appreciate the Analytical Listener’s highly rational thinking and value their focus on facts and data as an important component of decision-making.
AL.challenges: While working together, you can easily forget to consider how your ideas may affect other people or constituencies. Highly Analytical Listeners may be tempted to probe your ideas or play “devil’s advocate” to see how well they hold up.
AL.suggestions: While brainstorming, make an effort to consider how your solutions would affect all the people and constituencies involved. Try not to take the Analytical Listener’s probing as a statement of personal mistrust. Rather, value their inquiry as a tool that can help sharpen your thinking.

CL.atBest: You will typically enjoy good brainstorming rapport and feel you are on the same wavelength. Additionally, you can help Conceptual Listeners identify where they need more facts and data while offering your own insights from personal experience.
CL.challenges: You may tune out Conceptual Listeners when they ideate on too many possibilities that aren’t supported by facts or by what you’ve come to understand through your own experience. Together, you may consider numerous angles to approach a problem while forgetting to incorporate the “human” component.
CL.suggestions: When brainstorming, provide yourselves with clearly defined priorities and outcomes which can serve as a framework to better focus thinking & ideation. When you’re tempted to tune out Conceptual Listeners who seem unrealistic, instead ask them how they would get from here to there to help them think pragmatically.

name: The Balancer
intro: As a BALANCER, you rely on Conceptual Listening in combination with Analytical Listening. You listen equally for ideas and details. You will tend to tune out if either creative stimulation or well- presented facts are missing.
shortDescription: Balancers rely on Conceptual Listening in combination with Analytical Listening. They listen equally for ideas and details. They will tend to tune out if either creative stimulation or well- presented facts are missing.
strengths: Your profile is one of the rarest, and balances two very different habits simultaneously. You are adept at both big-picture thinking and fact-based analysis, bringing a strong listening combination that offers new approaches to chronic problems. You develop realistic, rational ideas, as opposed to mere inspirations that cannot be implemented. In meetings, you listen for fresh details that can lead to a new path toward finding the best answer to the situation at hand.
possibleChallenges: Because of your interest in ideas and facts, you may tend to be internally focused and not too interested in paying attention to other people, or incorporating their knowledge and experience. Since your internal focus is on ideas and facts, you may also tend to overlook how your own background and experiences can add value to creating solutions to the problem at hand. When solving a problem is of greater interest than idea generating, you will cease doing the latter. Conversely, when you find idea-generation to be especially interesting, you might lose sight of the problem.
insights: Establish time allotment for fact gathering and for idea-generation so that they can both be realized individually.
Clarify the problem early to make sure idea generation is aligned with a purpose.
Ask questions that will stimulate both details and fresh ideas.
Use non-verbal signals to show you are engaged with what others bring to an interaction.
Speak or write ideas that can be shared with others, and ask people for how their knowledge and experience can add value to the solution.

CV.challenges: • Because you value usefulness and practicality, you may tune out Connective Listeners who seem overly relational or emotionally biased in their thinking. • Connective Listeners may get bored by your combination of logical and abstract thinking, and perceive you as disconnected from the concerns of others.
CV.suggestions: • Outline clearly how your thinking will benefit or affect all the constituencies involved to help Connective Listeners see the value of your ideas and become advocates. • Resist the temptation to discard Connective Listeners who seem to soft or idealistic; they can bring a helpful “people” side to your practical thinking.

RV.challenges: • Reflective Listeners may tune out when you move too quickly into problem-solving or idea generating before they’ve had enough time to digest the full scope of the issue at hand. • You may simply ignore the Reflective Listener who doesn’t seem to be engaging in outward ideating and problem solving.
RV.suggestions: • Always give Reflective Listeners space and time to formulate their thoughts—they’ll bring useful insights that round out your thinking. • To encourage their engagement, you might ask Reflective Listeners about previous experiences that would be relevant to the issue at hand.

AL.challenges: • Together, you can become narrowly focused and bogged down in the details of problem-solving without forward movement. • You have the potential to work diligently side-by-side without considering how your own previous knowledge and experience—or that of others—could bring a helpful perspective to the issue at hand.
AL.suggestions: • When brainstorming, provide yourselves with clearly defined priorities and outcomes with a time-frame in mind so you don’t get stuck in the weeds. • Make a conscious effort to reflect on previous experiences that could offer new angles or solutions to the problem at hand.

CL.challenges: • While you’ll enjoy brainstorming with Conceptual Listeners, you probably won’t tolerate ideas that don’t have an obvious practical value. • Even when working productively together, you may tend to overlook how the ideas will affect other people.
CL.suggestions: • Instead of tuning out or playing “devil’s advocate” with Conceptual Listeners whose ideas you deem unrealistic, ask them how they would get from here to there to help them ground their thinking. • When brainstorming with Conceptual Listeners, make a concerted effort to determine how your ideas would impact other potentially affected groups.

name: The Tester
intro: As a TESTER, you rely on Conceptual Listening in combination with Analytical Listening and Connective Listening. You listen for ideas and how they can be used to help others set direction and find solutions. You will tend to tune out when there are no good options to exercise that could be of value to others seeking a path forward.
shortDescription: Testers rely on Conceptual Listening in combination with Analytical Listening and Connective Listening. They listen for ideas and how they can be used to help others set direction and find solutions. They will tend to tune out when there are no good options to exercise that could be of value to others seeking a path forward.
strengths: You generate ideas with an ear toward the context in which the ideas will be used and who will use them. You’re open to fresh ideas and options, but you also pay attention to the facts and data. You’re committed to finding practical solutions that appeal to others—not just yourself—and you’re also likely to collaborate with others to evaluate ideas and solutions because you’re interested in the experiences and knowledge that others bring to the table.
possibleChallenges: With a lower preference for Reflective listening, you may tend to overlook your own experience and knowledge that can add perspective for others. In fact, you may be impatient toward others who tend to be more reflective and introspective. You might move too quickly into action when deliberation is called for. Additionally, you might have a bias toward novelty rather than what has worked in the past. While brainstorming, you may occasionally struggle to stay focused when there are a lot of people, facts, and ideas to keep track of.
insights: Pause to consider unintended consequences that might occur before making comments and decisions.
Identify previous experiences that might have similarities that can add insights into to the present situation.
Ask others for feedback about proposed ideas and decisions.
Develop lists of questions that need to be answered before moving ahead, share them, and do not make any decisions until they are addressed.
Allot time for Reflective listeners to reflect and form their thoughts.

CV.atBest: You will tend to respect and the openness and “people focus” Connective Listeners bring to interactions, and they can help you ensure your ideas have factored in all constituencies. You, in turn, can offer imaginative ideas and practical solutions that will help Connective Listeners bring their people-oriented inspirations into reality.
CV.challenges: Because you value innovation and practical solutions, you may tune out Connective Listeners who seem overly relational or emotionally biased in their thinking. While you do tend to consider others in your decision making, Connective Listeners may disregard you when your interest in abstract and logical thinking takes over for extended periods.
CV.suggestions: If you find yourself getting too abstract, outline clearly how your thinking will benefit or affect all the constituencies involved to help Connective Listeners see the value of your ideas and become advocates. Resist the temptation to discard Connective Listeners who seem too soft or idealistic; they can help ensure you’ve considered the needs of everyone potentially affected by your ideas.

RV.atBest: You will appreciate the personal knowledge and thoughtful perspectives Reflective Listeners bring to interactions. They will appreciate your innovative thinking and will trust you to vet their ideas for feasibility.
RV.challenges: Reflective Listeners may tune out when you move too quickly into problem-solving or idea generating before they’ve had enough time to digest the full scope of the issue at hand. You may feel impatient or uncomfortable when a Reflective Listener doesn’t seem to be outwardly engaging in the unfolding problem solving or ideation.
RV.suggestions: Always give Reflective Listeners space and time to formulate their thoughts—they’ll bring useful insights that round out your thinking. To encourage their engagement, you might ask Reflective Listeners about previous experiences that would be relevant to the issue at hand.

AL.atBest: You will work well together, sharing a mutual respect for facts, data, and practical methods of implementation. Additionally, you will bring a slightly more “imaginative” side to the conversation and help Analytical Listeners find interesting new ways to apply their findings and consider how they might affect others.
AL.challenges: While you both appreciate the investigation of relevant facts, you will personally move toward decision- making more quickly, and may grow impatient with Analytical Listeners who want to keep deliberating in the details. Analytical Listeners may perceive you as too eager to move forward to test ideas without all the relevant details figured out.
AL.suggestions: When brainstorming, provide yourselves with clearly defined priorities and outcomes so that the depth of information required before decision-making is agreed upon at the outset. Since it likely won’t be a first impulse for either of you, try to make a conscious effort to stop and consider how lessons from previous experiences could inform the current issue you’re addressing.

CL.atBest: You will tend to work well together with your mutual appreciation for new ideas and possibilities. Additionally, you will bring a scrutinizing eye to the Conceptual Listener's inspirations and help vet them for usefulness and practicality while keeping other constituencies in the back of your mind.
CL.challenges: While you’ll enjoy ideating on novel possibilities with Conceptual Listeners, you’ll likely tune out ideas that aren’t backed up with some facts or evidence. Together, you have the potential to get carried away ideating on possibilities that may be backed by logic and facts, but aren’t necessarily grounded in your own practical experience.
CL.suggestions: Instead of tuning out Conceptual Listeners whose ideas aren’t backed by logic, ask them how they would get from here to there to help them ground their thinking. When brainstorming with Conceptual Listeners, you’ll naturally make sure ideas are backed by facts, but make a concerted effort to also weigh ideas against the knowledge you’ve gained through practical experience.

name: The Assessor
intro: As an ASSESSOR, you rely on Conceptual Listening in combination with Analytical Listening and Reflective Listening. You weigh new ideas against what is known and experienced. You will tend to tune out if there is too much emotion without clear and rigorous thought.
shortDescription: Assessors rely on Conceptual Listening in combination with Analytical Listening and Reflective Listening. They weigh new ideas against what is known and experienced. They will tend to tune out if there is too much emotion without clear and rigorous thought.
strengths: As a listener, you tune into ideas, but not for ones without relevance. With a pragmatic ear, you listen for what is being said, rather than who is saying it, and you use facts and data rather than instinct or opinion to evaluate ideas. You balance creativity with personal knowledge and experience to determine the value of possible solutions. You also understand complex issues and get to the bottom of them, often with fresh ideas of your own.
possibleChallenges: With a low preference for Connective listening, you may tend to overlook the opinions and needs of other people or departments. You may also strongly defend your own ideas and present them as fact. You can grow impatient when required to verbalize a satisfactory internal conversation. Because you listen for facts over feelings, you may miss the emotional dimension of a communication exchange. Your questions may seem impersonal and intellectual to those with a more relational interest.
insights: Learn about the people you’ll be listening to before an interaction.
Develop questions that will surface their interests and concerns.
Enter an interaction with the intention to learn rather than to tell or display your own knowledge.
Share ideas and thoughts rather than assuming others can read your mind.
Watch for a display of feelings from others – acknowledge and respond to them.

CV.atBest: You will offer practical, measured thinking and imagination that can help bring the Connective Listener’s people-oriented inspirations into reality. Connective Listeners can show you the different ways your ideas and proposed processes will affect various constituencies in ways you might not have considered.
CV.challenges: Because you value relevance and practicality, you may tune out Connective Listeners who seem overly relational or emotionally biased in their thinking. Connective Listeners may get bored by your combination of logical and abstract thinking, and perceive you as aloof to the concerns of others.
CV.suggestions: Outline clearly how your thinking will benefit or affect all the constituencies involved to help Connective Listeners see the value of your ideas and become advocates. Resist the temptation to discard Connective Listeners who seem too soft or idealistic; they can bring a helpful perspective to your practical thinking and show how it might affect other teams or departments.

RV.atBest: You will appreciate the personal knowledge and thoughtful perspectives Reflective Listeners bring to interactions. They will appreciate your innovative thinking and trust you to vet their ideas for feasibility.
RV.challenges: Reflective Listeners may tune out when you move too quickly into problem-solving or idea generating before they’ve had enough time to digest the full scope of the issue at hand. You may simply ignore the Reflective Listener who doesn’t seem to be engaging in outward ideating and problem solving.
RV.suggestions: Always give Reflective Listeners space and time to formulate their thoughts—they’ll bring useful insights that round out your thinking. To encourage their engagement, you might ask Reflective Listeners about previous experiences that would be relevant to the issue at hand.

AL.atBest: You will tend to work well together, sharing a mutual respect for unbiased facts, data, and practical methods of implementation. Additionally, you will bring a slightly more “imaginative” side to the conversation with thoughtful considerations that will likely help Analytical Listeners find interesting new ways to apply their findings.
AL.challenges: Together, you can become narrowly focused and bogged down in the details of problem-solving without significant forward movement or consideration of how your efforts will be affecting others. You also have the capacity to work diligently side-by-side without consulting each other and sharing the information needed to coordinate effectively.
AL.suggestions: When brainstorming, provide yourselves with clearly defined priorities and outcomes with a time-frame in mind so don’t get stuck in the weeds. Regularly communicate your efforts with each other to make sure you’re still on the “same page” and coordinating effectively.

CL.atBest: You will tend to work well together with your mutual appreciation for new ideas and possibilities. Additionally, you will bring a scrutinizing eye to the Conceptual Listener’s inspirations and help assess their usefulness and practicality.
CL.challenges: While you’ll enjoy brainstorming with Conceptual Listeners, you may be critical of ideas that contradict what you already know to be true from experience, or that don’t have an obvious practical value. Even when brainstorming productively together, you may tend to mutually ignore how the ideas will affect other people.
CL.suggestions: When you deem a Conceptual Listener’s thinking to be unrealistic, ask them how they would get from “here” to “there” and see what ideas they come up with. When brainstorming with Conceptual Listeners, make a concerted effort to determine how your ideas would impact other potentially affected groups. NON-DOMINANT LISTENING

name: The Flexer
intro: As a FLEXER, you rely on all 4 listening habits without a preference for any one over another. You listen for everything that is available and change your listening habit quickly and often according to the situation and the people. At times you may feel overwhelmed by the amount of information “coming at you” and can then tend to tune out when additional information is presented.
shortDescription: Flexers rely on all 4 listening habits without a preference for any one over another. They listen for everything that is available and change their listening habit quickly and often according to the situation and the people. At times they may feel overwhelmed by the amount of information “coming at they” and can then tend to tune out when additional information is presented.
strengths: As a non-dominant listener, you are able to switch between listening styles with relative ease. You appreciate the diverse ways people present information, and are aware of both facts and feelings throughout an interaction. You pay as much attention to what is said as to who is speaking, and make people comfortable during an interaction by focusing on them.
possibleChallenges: Because you listen for all types of information, your brain may tire and shut off from trying to keep up with so much input. You can therefore grow impatient with people who do not speak or present ideas quickly enough. You can also tend to move quickly from one mode of listening to another without pausing to hear the nuances of information being presented. Additionally, you probably shift from being ruled by emotions or by reason with little warning, which can cause confusion in others.
insights: Ask consistent questions that indicate you’re focused and not jumping from one subject to another.
Be aware of different body language signals and mirror them back to individual speakers to show you are connected to them.
Be an active participant, not an observer of the interaction.
If you feel overwhelmed by all the information coming at you, make sure you are thinking big picture and not stuck in the details.
Watch your pace and slow down if you notice that you or others feel rushed.

CV.atBest: You will tend to get along easily with Connective Listeners, each appreciating the other’s natural ability to relate and connect. Connective Listeners will also likely admire your ability to communicate on the same wavelength with whomever is speaking, regardless of their style.
CV.challenges: Connective Listeners may become surprised or discomforted when you seem to suddenly switch from a relational mode into a different way of listening, thus shifting the conversational dynamic without warning. While you easily meet Connective Listeners where they are at the beginning of an interaction, you may tend to grow tired if you’re expected to keep the relational component going indefinitely.
CV.suggestions: Know what is important to discuss and stay with that. In general, when speaking with Connective Listeners, show how your thinking includes the consideration of other people and departments to help them more easily see the value of your ideas.

RV.atBest: You will tend to value and appreciate the Reflective Listener’s thoughtful considerations. Reflective Listeners will appreciate your ability to see when they have something to contribute but not push them when they’re still processing internally.
RV.challenges: Reflective Listeners may become frustrated when you shift your listening from rational to emotional and push the conversation in seemingly disparate directions. You may appreciate the valuable insights Reflective Listeners bring but be frustrated when they’re not forthcoming in how they arrived at their conclusions.
RV.suggestions: If you notice a Reflective Listener hasn’t spoken for a while but don’t want to push them, instead of asking directly what they think, try asking what previous experiences they’ve had that might be relevant to the situation under consideration. When speaking with Reflective Listeners, be transparent about which parts of your current thinking were borne out of your previous experiences to help them more readily connect in with the value of your thinking.

AL.atBest: You’ll tend to appreciate the straightforward approach of Analytical Listeners who set aside emotions and get to the essence of an issue, often citing relevant facts and data to underscore their thinking. Analytical Listeners will appreciate your ability to mirror their thinking and bridge it with the listening styles of others when needed.
AL.challenges: Analytical Listeners may interpret your initial receptivity to their thinking as an invitation to go further into detailed analysis, at which point you may grow tired and tune them out. Analytical Listeners may wonder why you suddenly aren’t following them anymore when you shift to another mode of listening.
AL.suggestions: Notice the moments when your listening shifts so you can determine if the shift was needed (i.e. you needed to start considering a different perspective) or you simply got bored and can afford to stay on track. In general, when speaking with Analytical Listeners, back your thoughts, ideas, and considerations with facts, details, or evidence that will more easily help them understand the value of your thinking.

CL.atBest: You will appreciate the creative ideas and possibilities Conceptual Listeners bring to the conversation, which helps expand your own ideating further. Conceptual Listeners will appreciate your receptivity to their thinking and the added contributions you bring from multiple perspectives.
CL.challenges: You may shift away from ideating just when the Conceptual Listener thinks you’re getting started, leaving them feeling that the process is incomplete. You may wonder how a Conceptual Listener can ideate at such length without placing equal focus on facts, details, personal experience, and consideration of other people and departments.
CL.suggestions: If you find yourself internally “shifting away” from the conversation, try asking the Conceptual Listener questions about what they’re saying, but from the point of view of your shifted listening focus (e.g., “What resources would we need to achieve what you’re describing?” or “How would these ideas affect other teams and departments?”). When brainstorming with Conceptual Listeners, provide yourselves with clearly defined priorities and outcomes which can serve as a framework within which to better focus thinking & ideation.
